AZD-8055.name: AZD-8055
AZD-8055.description: AZD8055 has been used in trials studying the treatment of Cancer, Lymphomas, Solid Tumors, MALIGNANT GLIOMA, and brainstem glioma, among others.

Abemaciclib.name: Abemaciclib
Abemaciclib.description: Abemaciclib is an antitumor agent that inhibits cyclin-dependent kinases 4 and 6 (CDK4/6), which are involved in cancer cell growth. It is approved for treating HR-positive, HER2-negative advanced or metastatic breast cancer, especially after failure of endocrine therapy. The drug can be used alone or in combination with other therapies. Key effects include increased progression-free survival and objective response rates in treated patients.
Abemaciclib.mechanism_of_action: Abemaciclib selectively inhibits cyclin-dependent kinases CDK4 and CDK6, preventing their interaction with D-type cyclins. This inhibition blocks phosphorylation of the retinoblastoma protein (Rb), maintaining its suppression of E2F transcription factors and halting cell cycle progression at the G1 phase. As a result, abemaciclib induces G1 cell cycle arrest and inhibits cellular proliferation, specifically in Rb-proficient cells. Compared to other CDK inhibitors, abemaciclib shows greater selectivity for CDK4.
Abemaciclib.pharmacodynamics: Abemaciclib, combined with fulvestrant, significantly improves progression-free survival in HR-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer patients compared to placebo. As a monotherapy, it leads to tumor shrinkage in 19.7% of patients for a median duration of 8.6 months. Its pharmacodynamic action involves inducing cell cycle arrest and demonstrating antitumor activity in preclinical models. Abemaciclib does not cause clinically significant changes in QTc interval based on patient and healthy volunteer studies.

Acetohexamide.name: Acetohexamide
Acetohexamide.description: A sulfonylurea hypoglycemic agent that is metabolized in the liver to 1-hydrohexamide. Acetohexamide has been discontinued in the US market.
Acetohexamide.mechanism_of_action: Sulfonylureas like acetohexamide bind to ATP-dependent potassium channels on pancreatic beta cells, inhibiting potassium efflux. This causes membrane depolarization, which opens voltage-gated calcium channels. The resulting increase in intracellular calcium triggers insulin granule fusion with the cell membrane. Ultimately, this leads to increased secretion of insulin.
Acetohexamide.pharmacodynamics: Acetohexamide is a first-generation sulfonylurea that lowers blood glucose by stimulating pancreatic beta cells to release insulin and enhancing insulin utilization. Its effectiveness depends on the presence of functional beta cells. The drug is less potent than chlorpropamide but more potent than tolbutamide, with similar hypoglycemic effects at equivalent doses. Clinical outcomes rely on adequate pancreatic insulin production.

Adagrasib.name: Adagrasib
Adagrasib.description: Adagrasib (MRTX849) is an oral small-molecule inhibitor targeting the KRAS^G12C mutation, developed by Mirati Therapeutics. It is mainly indicated for the treatment of locally advanced or metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) with KRAS^G12C mutations in patients who have received prior systemic therapy. Clinical studies have shown that adagrasib exhibits anti-tumor activity and an acceptable safety profile. The FDA granted accelerated approval for adagrasib in December 2022.
Adagrasib.mechanism_of_action: KRAS normally switches between active (GTP-bound) and inactive (GDP-bound) states to regulate cell growth. The KRAS^G12C mutation impairs GTP hydrolysis, keeping KRAS persistently active and driving uncontrolled cell proliferation. Adagrasib is a covalent inhibitor that selectively binds KRAS^G12C, locking it in the inactive GDP-bound state. This inhibits tumor growth in cancers with the KRAS^G12C mutation, with minimal off-target effects.
Adagrasib.pharmacodynamics: Adagrasib can cause concentration-dependent QTc interval prolongation, with a mean QTcF increase of 18 ms at steady-state with 600 mg twice daily dosing. The exposure-response and pharmacodynamic time course are not fully characterized. Clinically, adagrasib may also result in severe gastrointestinal adverse reactions, hepatotoxicity, and interstitial lung disease/pneumonitis. Careful monitoring for these adverse effects is recommended during treatment.

Afatinib.name: Afatinib
Afatinib.description: Afatinib (brand name Gilotrif) is an oral 4-anilinoquinazoline tyrosine kinase inhibitor. It is primarily indicated as a first-line treatment for patients with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) who have common EGFR mutations. The drug works by blocking the activity of EGFR, which helps slow cancer cell growth. Gilotrif is the first FDA-approved oncology product from Boehringer Ingelheim.
Afatinib.mechanism_of_action: Afatinib is an irreversible inhibitor of the ErbB family of receptors, including EGFR (ErbB1), HER2 (ErbB2), and HER4 (ErbB4). It covalently binds to their kinase domains, blocking autophosphorylation and downstream signaling. This inhibition is effective against tumors with certain activating EGFR mutations, such as exon 19 deletions and exon 21 L858R substitutions. Afatinib also inhibits proliferation in cell lines overexpressing HER2.
Afatinib.pharmacodynamics: Afatinib effectively inhibits ErbB receptor signaling, leading to tumor growth inhibition or regression in models with ErbB pathway deregulation. It is particularly effective in NSCLC tumors with common EGFR mutations (Del 19, L858R) and some less common mutations, but shows limited activity against exon 20 insertion mutations. Resistance often develops through the T790M mutation or MET amplification, with T790M present in about 50% of patients after progression. Afatinib does not cause significant QTc interval prolongation at therapeutic doses.

Aliskiren.name: Aliskiren
Aliskiren.description: Aliskiren is a renin inhibitor used to treat hypertension. It was developed by Speedel and Novartis and approved by the FDA in 2007. The drug effectively lowers blood pressure, either as monotherapy or combined with other antihypertensive medications. Its key effect is the reduction of blood pressure through inhibition of renin activity.
Aliskiren.mechanism_of_action: Aliskiren is a direct renin inhibitor that binds to renin's active site. This blocks the conversion of angiotensinogen to angiotensin I, preventing the formation of angiotensin II. As a result, vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sodium reabsorption are reduced. Ultimately, this leads to lower blood pressure.
Aliskiren.pharmacodynamics: Aliskiren lowers blood pressure by directly inhibiting renin activity. This inhibition disrupts the renin-angiotensin system, resulting in decreased blood pressure. Clinically, this reduces the risk of fatal and nonfatal cardiovascular events such as stroke and myocardial infarction.

Allantoin.name: Allantoin
Allantoin.description: Allantoin is a naturally occurring compound in the human body and a metabolic product of uric acid. It is commonly used in over-the-counter cosmetic and personal care products, such as creams, shampoos, and oral hygiene items. Main indications include promoting skin healing and soothing irritation. Key effects include enhancing wound healing and providing skin protection.
Allantoin.mechanism_of_action: Allantoin's exact mechanism of action is not fully established. Studies suggest it promotes wound healing by enhancing tissue repair processes. In animal models, allantoin increases vasodilation, inflammatory cell activity, angiogenesis, fibroblast proliferation, and collagen deposition at wound sites. These effects collectively support faster and improved skin regeneration.
Allantoin.pharmacodynamics: Allantoin's pharmacodynamic properties are not formally substantiated by well-controlled studies. However, available data indicate it has moisturizing and keratolytic effects. It increases extracellular matrix water content and promotes desquamation of dead skin cells. These actions may enhance cell proliferation and support wound healing.

Allopurinol.name: Allopurinol
Allopurinol.description: Allopurinol is a xanthine oxidase inhibitor used to lower urate levels in the body. Its main indication is the treatment of chronic gout, a condition caused by monosodium urate crystal deposition in joints. Allopurinol effectively reduces uric acid production, helping to prevent gout attacks. It was first FDA-approved in 1966 and is widely available from multiple manufacturers.
Allopurinol.mechanism_of_action: Allopurinol is a structural analog of hypoxanthine and is metabolized to oxypurinol, its active form. Both allopurinol and oxypurinol inhibit xanthine oxidase, the enzyme responsible for converting hypoxanthine and xanthine to uric acid. This inhibition lowers serum and urine uric acid levels, reducing gout symptoms and tophi formation. Increased hypoxanthine and xanthine are more soluble and are excreted by the kidneys, minimizing the risk of renal complications.
Allopurinol.pharmacodynamics: Allopurinol reduces uric acid production by inhibiting its biochemical formation. This leads to lower urate levels in the body. Clinically, it alleviates symptoms of gout such as joint pain, inflammation, and swelling. Patients experience relief from painful tophi and improved joint mobility.

Alpelisib.name: Alpelisib
Alpelisib.description: Alpelisib is an oral PI3K inhibitor marketed as Piqray. It is primarily indicated for HR-positive, HER2-negative, PIK3CA-mutated advanced or metastatic breast cancer, used in combination with fulvestrant for postmenopausal women and men. The drug selectively inhibits the PI3K p110α subunit, blocking cancer cell growth and survival. Alpelisib is also FDA-approved for treating PIK3CA-Related Overgrowth Spectrum (PROS) in adults and children needing systemic therapy.
Alpelisib.mechanism_of_action: Alpelisib is a selective inhibitor of phosphatidylinositol-3-kinase alpha (PI3Kα). PI3Kα mediates cell proliferation via growth factor-tyrosine kinase pathways. In certain cancers, mutations in the p110α subunit cause PI3Kα hyperactivation. Alpelisib targets and inhibits PI3Kα, reducing cancer cell growth.
Alpelisib.pharmacodynamics: Alpelisib does not prolong the QTcF interval. It shows dose-dependent efficacy, with a 51% greater benefit at 200 mg daily compared to 100 mg, and a 22% advantage at 300 mg once daily over 150 mg twice daily. Patients needing lower doses may achieve better outcomes with twice daily dosing. These findings guide optimal dosing strategies for improved clinical benefit.

Altretamine.name: Altretamine
Altretamine.description: An alkylating agent proposed as an antineoplastic. It also acts as a chemosterilant for male houseflies and other insects.
Altretamine.mechanism_of_action: Altretamine is classified as an alkylating anti-neoplastic agent. It is metabolized via N-demethylation to produce active alkylating species. These metabolites damage tumor cells by interfering with their DNA. The exact cytotoxic mechanism remains unclear.
Altretamine.pharmacodynamics: Altretamine is an antineoplastic agent with an unclear mechanism of cytotoxicity. Although structurally similar to alkylating agents, it does not demonstrate alkylating activity in standard tests. Its metabolites are necessary for cytotoxic effects and can form covalent adducts with DNA, though the clinical significance of this is uncertain. Altretamine has shown efficacy in treating certain ovarian tumors resistant to classical alkylating agents.

Amsacrine.name: Amsacrine
Amsacrine.description: This drug is an aminoacridine derivative and a potent intercalating antineoplastic agent. It is mainly indicated for acute leukemias and malignant lymphomas, with limited effectiveness against solid tumors. It is often used in combination chemotherapy regimens. Key effects include consistent myelosuppression and potential cardiotoxicity.
Amsacrine.mechanism_of_action: Amsacrine acts by intercalating into DNA, with a preference for A-T base pairs, and externally binding to DNA. It inhibits topoisomerase II, leading to double-stranded DNA breaks. Its cytotoxic effects are most pronounced during the S phase of the cell cycle when topoisomerase II levels are highest. Rapidly dividing cells are more sensitive to amsacrine than resting cells.
Amsacrine.pharmacodynamics: Amsacrine is a potent intercalating antineoplastic agent primarily effective against acute leukemias and malignant lymphomas. It shows limited efficacy in treating solid tumors. The drug is often used in combination chemotherapy regimens. Key adverse effects include consistent but manageable myelosuppression and cardiotoxicity.

Anastrozole.name: Anastrozole
Anastrozole.description: Anastrozole is a non-steroidal aromatase inhibitor used primarily to treat estrogen-responsive breast cancer in postmenopausal women. It works by lowering circulating estrogen levels, which helps slow or stop tumor growth. Compared to steroidal AIs, anastrozole has fewer steroid-related side effects such as weight gain and acne. It is preferred over older drugs like tamoxifen due to its improved efficacy and safety profile.
Anastrozole.mechanism_of_action: Anastrozole selectively and competitively inhibits the aromatase enzyme, which is responsible for converting androgens to estrogens in tissues like the adrenal glands, liver, and fat. By blocking aromatase, anastrozole reduces estrogen production, especially in postmenopausal women. Lower estrogen levels suppress the growth of hormone receptor-positive breast tumors. This mechanism makes anastrozole effective in treating estrogen-dependent breast cancer.
Anastrozole.pharmacodynamics: Anastrozole inhibits the conversion of androgens to estrogen, lowering circulating estrogen levels and helping to treat estrogen-dependent breast cancers. It reduces serum estradiol by about 70% within 24 hours and maintains suppression for several days after stopping therapy. An increased risk of ischemic cardiovascular events has been observed, especially in patients with pre-existing heart disease. Long-term use may decrease bone mineral density, so monitoring is recommended.

Anethole trithione.name: Anethole trithione
Anethole trithione.description: Anethole trithione (ATT) is a drug primarily used to increase salivary secretion for treating dry mouth (xerostomia). It has also shown potential in inhibiting carcinogenesis and is used as adjunctive therapy for conditions like cholecystitis, gallstones, indigestion, and hepatitis. ATT is marketed in select countries such as France, Germany, and China. Its exact mechanisms are not fully understood, and formal indications are limited.
Anethole trithione.mechanism_of_action: Anethole trithione (ATT) increases salivary secretion by upregulating muscarinic receptors on salivary acinar cells, enhancing their response to stimulation. It also promotes the release of substance P and alpha-calcitonin gene-related peptide, which may modulate various physiological functions. In the liver, ATT boosts glutathione levels and related enzyme activities, supporting antioxidant defense and detoxification. Additionally, ATT may reduce carcinogenesis by enhancing detoxification of carcinogens and increasing antioxidant enzyme activity in target organs.
Anethole trithione.pharmacodynamics: Anethol trithione (ATT) has high lipophilicity but very low water solubility, which restricts its absorption and dissolution. It is rapidly metabolized to 4-hydroxy-anethole trithione (ATX), which has similar pharmacological effects. Due to these properties, ATT exhibits low plasma concentrations and limited oral bioavailability. Ongoing research focuses on improving its delivery to enhance clinical effectiveness.

Artesunate.name: Artesunate
Artesunate.description: Artesunate is an antimalarial drug derived from artemisinin. It is primarily indicated for the initial treatment of severe malaria and is recommended as first-line therapy by the World Health Organization. Artesunate is more hydrophilic than artemisinin, improving its effectiveness in severe cases. It received FDA approval on May 26, 2020.
Artesunate.mechanism_of_action: Artesunate is converted to its active form, dihydroartemisinin (DHA). DHA's endoperoxide bridge reacts with heme in the parasite, generating free radicals. These free radicals inhibit protein and nucleic acid synthesis in Plasmodium during erythrocytic stages. Additionally, they can alkylate key parasitic proteins like calcium ATPase and EXP1, disrupting parasite function.
Artesunate.pharmacodynamics: Artesunate is an artemisinin derivative that is metabolized to DHA, producing free radicals that disrupt Plasmodium parasite function. It acts rapidly but has a short duration due to its brief half-life. The drug has a moderate therapeutic index. Patients should be warned about possible post-treatment hemolytic anemia and hypersensitivity reactions.

Asciminib.name: Asciminib
Asciminib.description: Asciminib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor used to treat chronic-phase Philadelphia chromosome-positive chronic myeloid leukemia (Ph+ CML). It specifically inhibits the ABL1 kinase activity of the BCR-ABL1 fusion protein, including forms with the treatment-resistant T315I mutation. Unlike other TKIs, asciminib acts as an allosteric inhibitor by binding to the myristoyl pocket, locking the protein in an inactive state. It was FDA-approved in October 2021 under the brand name Scemblix.
Asciminib.mechanism_of_action: Asciminib is an allosteric inhibitor targeting the BCR-ABL1 fusion protein, which drives chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) through elevated tyrosine kinase activity. It specifically binds to the myristoyl pocket of the ABL1 portion of BCR-ABL1. This binding locks the protein in an inactive conformation, blocking its oncogenic signaling. As a result, asciminib inhibits CML cell proliferation.
Asciminib.pharmacodynamics: Asciminib inhibits a key oncogenic protein driving chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) proliferation. It is effective against both standard CML and the treatment-resistant T315I mutation when used at higher doses. Common adverse effects include myelosuppression, such as thrombocytopenia and neutropenia, requiring frequent lab monitoring and possible dose adjustments. Pancreatic and cardiovascular toxicities are also potential risks, necessitating ongoing monitoring during therapy.

Ataluren.name: Ataluren
Ataluren.description: Ataluren is an oral medication designed to treat genetic disorders caused by nonsense mutations. It is approved in Europe for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy in ambulatory patients aged 5 years and older with nonsense mutations in the dystrophin gene. The drug helps restore dystrophin protein production, potentially slowing disease progression. Ataluren is not approved by the FDA or Health Canada.
Ataluren.mechanism_of_action: Ataluren promotes ribosomal readthrough of premature stop codons in mRNA, enabling the production of full-length, functional proteins despite nonsense mutations. It shows maximal activity at the UGA stop codon and does not affect mRNA levels. Ataluren is more potent than gentamicin in stimulating readthrough and modulates termination efficiency at these codons. This mechanism helps restore protein synthesis in conditions caused by nonsense mutations.

Auranofin.name: Auranofin
Auranofin.description: Auranofin is a gold salt classified as an antirheumatic agent. It is mainly indicated for the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis. The drug suppresses inflammation and stimulates cell-mediated immunity. Auranofin also induces heme oxygenase 1, an enzyme with anti-inflammatory effects.
Auranofin.mechanism_of_action: Auranofin is a gold salt used to reduce joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis by suppressing immune responses, including inhibition of phagocytosis and release of inflammatory mediators. Its main molecular target is thioredoxin reductase, an enzyme involved in cellular redox homeostasis. By inhibiting thioredoxin reductase, auranofin increases oxidative stress and promotes apoptosis, particularly in tumor cells. The drug binds irreversibly to thiol and selenol groups, enhancing free radical production and affecting immune cell activation.
Auranofin.pharmacodynamics: Auranofin is a gold salt used to treat inflammatory arthritis. It is typically considered a second-line therapy when NSAIDs and corticosteroids are insufficient. Auranofin modulates immune responses to reduce joint inflammation and slow disease progression. Clinical outcomes include decreased pain and improved joint function in patients with refractory arthritis.

Belumosudil.name: Belumosudil
Belumosudil.description: Belumosudil is a selective ROCK2 inhibitor approved for the treatment of chronic graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) in patients who have failed at least two prior systemic therapies. It works by modulating immune cell balance, reducing inflammation caused by donor T-cells attacking recipient tissues after stem cell transplantation. The drug helps resolve immune dysregulation and dampens the potentially fatal inflammatory response in GVHD. Belumosudil is marketed as Rezurock in the US and RHOLISTIQ in Canada for both adult and pediatric patients aged 12 and older.
Belumosudil.mechanism_of_action: Belumosudil inhibits Rho-associated coiled-coil kinase 2 (ROCK2), a key enzyme involved in immune and fibrotic disease pathways. By blocking ROCK2, belumosudil reduces pro-inflammatory Th17 cell activity and increases regulatory T-cell levels. This modulation occurs through altered phosphorylation of STAT3 and STAT5 proteins. The overall effect is improved immune regulation and reduced tissue inflammation in chronic GVHD.
Belumosudil.pharmacodynamics: Belumosudil inhibits pro-fibrotic and pro-inflammatory processes, helping prevent and treat graft-versus-host disease damage. Its mechanism and animal studies indicate a risk of embryo-fetal toxicity. Exposure during pregnancy may harm a developing fetus. Patients of reproductive potential should use effective contraception during treatment and for one week after the last dose.

Bexarotene.name: Bexarotene
Bexarotene.description: Bexarotene (Targretin) is an antineoplastic agent. It is FDA-approved for the treatment of Cutaneous T cell lymphoma. Off-label, it has been used for lung cancer, breast cancer, and Kaposi's sarcoma. Its key effect is inhibiting tumor cell growth.
Bexarotene.mechanism_of_action: Bexarotene selectively binds to and activates retinoid X receptor (RXR) subtypes: RXRα, RXRβ, and RXRγ. Activation of these nuclear receptors regulates gene expression involved in cell differentiation and proliferation. While the precise mechanism in cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (CTCL) is unknown, bexarotene shows clinical activity across all stages of the disease.
Bexarotene.pharmacodynamics: Bexarotene is a retinoid that selectively activates retinoid X receptors (RXRs), which regulate gene expression involved in cellular differentiation and proliferation. It is indicated for treating cutaneous T-cell lymphoma in patients refractory to prior systemic therapy. Bexarotene inhibits the growth of certain tumor cell lines and induces tumor regression in animal models. Its clinical effects are mediated through RXR activation and modulation of cellular growth pathways.

Bicalutamide.name: Bicalutamide
Bicalutamide.description: Bicalutamide is an oral non-steroidal anti-androgen used primarily to treat prostate cancer. It works by binding to androgen receptors, blocking the effects of male hormones that promote cancer growth. The drug consists of a 50:50 mixture of (R)- and (S)-bicalutamide enantiomers. Its key effect is to inhibit androgen-driven tumor progression.
Bicalutamide.mechanism_of_action: Bicalutamide is an androgen receptor antagonist. It competes with androgens for binding to androgen receptors. This blocks androgen action from both adrenal and testicular sources. As a result, it inhibits the growth of normal and cancerous prostate tissue.
Bicalutamide.pharmacodynamics: Bicalutamide is a nonsteroidal anti-androgen used mainly for prostate cancer treatment. It selectively blocks androgen receptors, inhibiting androgen-driven growth of normal and cancerous prostate tissue. By reducing androgen effects, it helps control prostate cancer progression. However, antiandrogen monotherapy with bicalutamide is generally less effective than surgical or chemical castration.

Bimatoprost.name: Bimatoprost
Bimatoprost.description: Bimatoprost is a synthetic prostaglandin analog used in ophthalmic solutions and implants. Its main indications are the treatment of ocular hypertension and glaucoma by reducing intraocular pressure. Additionally, it is used to treat eyelash hypotrichosis by promoting eyelash growth. Key effects include lowering eye pressure and enhancing eyelash length and thickness.
Bimatoprost.mechanism_of_action: Bimatoprost mimics prostaglandin F2α effects by acting as a prostamide analog. Its main action is to increase aqueous humor outflow through the trabecular meshwork and uveoscleral pathways. This reduces intraocular pressure and lowers the risk of optic nerve damage. Bimatoprost does not influence aqueous humor production.
Bimatoprost.pharmacodynamics: High intraocular pressure increases the risk of optic nerve damage and visual field loss in glaucoma and ocular hypertension. Bimatoprost rapidly lowers intraocular pressure, reducing the risk of vision loss. The drug may also cause gradual changes in eyelid, iris, and eyelash pigmentation, as well as increased eyelash growth and thickness. Patients should be informed of these possible cosmetic effects, especially with one-eye treatment.

Bimiralisib.name: Bimiralisib
Bimiralisib.description: Bimiralisib is an investigational drug being studied for cancer treatment. It is currently in clinical trials for use in metastatic HER2-negative and triple-negative breast cancer. The drug is being evaluated in combination with eribulin. Key effects and efficacy are still under investigation.

Binimetinib.name: Binimetinib
Binimetinib.description: Binimetinib (Mektovi) is an oral MEK 1/2 inhibitor. It is approved for use in combination with encorafenib for patients with unresectable or metastatic melanoma harboring BRAF V600E or V600K mutations. The combination targets abnormal signaling pathways in cancer cells, helping to slow tumor growth. Approval is based on its effectiveness in treating specific BRAF-mutant melanoma.
Binimetinib.mechanism_of_action: Binimetinib is a reversible, non-ATP competitive inhibitor of MEK1 and MEK2 kinases. By blocking MEK1/2 activity, it prevents downstream activation of the ERK signaling pathway. This inhibition disrupts tumor cell proliferation and reduces production of inflammatory cytokines such as IL-1, IL-6, and TNF. The drug targets the RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK pathway, which is often upregulated in various cancers.
Binimetinib.pharmacodynamics: Binimetinib inhibits ERK phosphorylation and reduces tumor cell viability in BRAF-mutant melanoma models by targeting the MEK enzyme, which also regulates inflammatory cytokine production. In vivo, binimetinib suppresses tumor growth and ERK activity in BRAF-mutant xenografts. Combined with encorafenib, binimetinib shows enhanced anti-tumor effects, delays resistance, and further inhibits tumor growth compared to either agent alone. Clinically, binimetinib at recommended doses does not cause significant QT prolongation.

Brimonidine.name: Brimonidine
Brimonidine.description: Brimonidine is a selective alpha-2 adrenergic agonist used primarily to lower intraocular pressure in the treatment of glaucoma and ocular hypertension. It works by reducing aqueous humor production and increasing uveoscleral outflow. Brimonidine is also approved as a topical gel for treating persistent facial erythema in adults with rosacea. It is considered effective and safe, especially for patients at risk for cardiopulmonary side effects, and has fewer ocular allergic reactions compared to similar drugs.
Brimonidine.mechanism_of_action: Brimonidine is an alpha-2 adrenoceptor agonist that lowers intraocular pressure (IOP) by inhibiting adenylyl cyclase, reducing cyclic AMP, and decreasing aqueous humor production. With chronic use, it also increases uveoscleral outflow. By reducing IOP, brimonidine helps prevent glaucomatous optic neuropathy and visual field loss. When applied to the skin, it causes vasoconstriction, effectively treating facial erythema in rosacea.
Brimonidine.pharmacodynamics: Brimonidine is a highly selective alpha-2 adrenergic agonist, providing significant ocular hypotensive effects with reduced risk of systemic and alpha-1 mediated ocular side effects. Clinically, it lowers intraocular pressure by about 23% within hours and maintains efficacy in glaucoma and ocular hypertension for up to one year. Brimonidine also acts as a potent vasoconstrictor, improving facial erythema in rosacea and showing anti-inflammatory properties in preclinical models. Systemic administration may cause cardiovascular effects, and experimental data suggest possible neuroprotective benefits, though further research is needed to confirm these outcomes.

Brivudine.name: Brivudine
Brivudine.description: Brivudine is used in the treatment of herpes zoster. Although not approved in the U.S. or Canada, it is approved in several European countries.

Busulfan.name: Busulfan
Busulfan.description: Busulfan is a bifunctional alkylating agent with selective immunosuppressive effects on bone marrow. It is primarily used for the palliative treatment of chronic myeloid leukemia. While it provides symptomatic relief, it does not induce permanent remission. Busulfan is also classified as a known carcinogen.
Busulfan.mechanism_of_action: Busulfan is an alkylating agent that forms reactive carbonium ions after hydrolysis. These ions alkylate DNA, primarily creating guanine-adenine intrastrand crosslinks via an SN2 reaction. The resulting DNA damage disrupts replication and transcription, leading to loss of nucleic acid function. Because the crosslinks are irreparable, affected cells undergo apoptosis.
Busulfan.pharmacodynamics: Busulfan is an alkylating antineoplastic agent that disrupts DNA by cross-linking guanine bases, preventing DNA replication and cell division. It also causes miscoding by adding alkyl groups to DNA, leading to cell death. Busulfan acts in a cell cycle-nonspecific manner, affecting cancer cells regardless of their replication stage. Overexpression of MGST2 may confer resistance to busulfan, though its exact metabolic role is unclear.

Canagliflozin.name: Canagliflozin
Canagliflozin.description: Canagliflozin (Invokana) is an oral SGLT2 inhibitor used to treat type 2 diabetes mellitus. It is indicated for improving blood sugar control alongside diet and exercise. Additionally, it is approved to reduce the risk of cardiovascular events in patients with type 2 diabetes. Canagliflozin is notable as the first oral antidiabetic drug approved for cardiovascular event prevention in this population.
Canagliflozin.mechanism_of_action: Canagliflozin inhibits the sodium-glucose co-transporter 2 (SGLT2) in the proximal renal tubules. This blocks glucose reabsorption from the urine back into the bloodstream. As a result, more glucose is excreted in the urine, lowering blood glucose levels. The drug also decreases the renal threshold for glucose.
Canagliflozin.pharmacodynamics: Canagliflozin increases urinary glucose excretion by lowering the renal threshold for glucose in a dose-dependent manner. This leads to reduced blood glucose levels and improved glycemic control in patients with type 2 diabetes. Effective long-term glycemic control helps prevent cardiovascular events, such as stroke and myocardial infarction, which are more common in diabetic patients due to vascular damage from chronic hyperglycemia.

Capmatinib.name: Capmatinib
Capmatinib.description: Capmatinib (brand name Tabrecta) is a small molecule kinase inhibitor targeting the c-Met receptor. It is primarily indicated for the treatment of non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) in patients whose tumors have MET exon 14 skipping mutations, confirmed by an FDA-approved test. Capmatinib works by inhibiting aberrant c-Met signaling, which is implicated in cancer progression. Its approval was granted under the FDA's accelerated program, with continued approval dependent on further clinical benefit confirmation.
Capmatinib.mechanism_of_action: Capmatinib is a selective inhibitor of the c-Met receptor tyrosine kinase. It blocks phosphorylation of both wild-type and mutant c-Met, including those with MET exon 14 skipping mutations. By inhibiting c-Met activation, capmatinib prevents downstream signaling involved in tumor cell proliferation and survival. This action targets c-Met-dependent cancers such as non-small cell lung cancer.
Capmatinib.pharmacodynamics: Capmatinib inhibits c-Met, a receptor tyrosine kinase involved in cancer cell proliferation, particularly in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). It can cause photosensitivity, so patients should use sunscreen and protective clothing during treatment. Serious cases of interstitial lung disease or pneumonitis have been reported and may be fatal. Capmatinib should be stopped immediately if lung symptoms occur and permanently discontinued if no other cause is found.

Ciclopirox.name: Ciclopirox
Ciclopirox.description: Ciclopirox olamine is a synthetic antifungal medication used topically for skin infections. It is indicated for the treatment of superficial mycoses, particularly Tinea versicolor. The drug works by inhibiting the growth of fungi on the skin. Common brand names include Batrafen, Loprox, Mycoster, Penlac, and Stieprox.
Ciclopirox.mechanism_of_action: Ciclopirox acts primarily by chelating metal cations like Fe³⁺ and Al³⁺, which inhibits key fungal enzymes and disrupts mitochondrial electron transport and energy production. It also alters fungal plasma membranes, leading to disorganization of internal cell structures. Additionally, ciclopirox inhibits 5-lipoxygenase and cyclooxygenase, contributing to its anti-inflammatory effects. The drug may further interfere with DNA repair, cell division, and intracellular transport in fungal cells.
Ciclopirox.pharmacodynamics: Ciclopirox is a broad-spectrum antifungal with additional antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties. It acts by binding trivalent cations, inhibiting enzyme co-factors essential for fungal survival. Clinically, it shows fungistatic or fungicidal activity against various fungi and is effective against many Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Its anti-inflammatory effects are due to inhibition of prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis via suppression of 5-lipoxygenase and cyclooxygenase.

Cinacalcet.name: Cinacalcet
Cinacalcet.description: Cinacalcet is a calcimimetic medication marketed as Sensipar® or Mimpara®. It is primarily used to treat hyperparathyroidism caused by parathyroid tumors or chronic kidney disease. The drug works by lowering parathyroid hormone levels, thereby helping to control calcium and phosphate balance in the body.
Cinacalcet.mechanism_of_action: Cinacalcet increases the sensitivity of calcium-sensing receptors on parathyroid cells to extracellular calcium. This enhanced sensitivity inhibits parathyroid hormone (PTH) secretion. Lower PTH levels lead to a decrease in serum calcium. The drug’s main target is the calcium-sensing receptor.
Cinacalcet.pharmacodynamics: Cinacalcet acts as a calcimimetic, increasing the sensitivity of the calcium-sensing receptor to extracellular calcium. This leads to reduced parathyroid hormone (PTH) levels and lowers serum calcium and phosphorus in patients with secondary hyperparathyroidism due to chronic kidney disease. Clinically, cinacalcet helps prevent bone disease and systemic complications by improving bone turnover and reducing bone fibrosis. Reductions in PTH with cinacalcet are associated with favorable effects on bone-specific alkaline phosphatase and overall bone health.

Clobetasol propionate.name: Clobetasol propionate
Clobetasol propionate.description: Clobetasol propionate is a potent topical corticosteroid derived from prednisolone. It is primarily indicated for treating inflammatory skin conditions such as psoriasis and eczema. The drug has strong glucocorticoid receptor specificity, leading to superior anti-inflammatory effects compared to other corticosteroids like fluocinonide. Clobetasol propionate received FDA approval in 1985.
Clobetasol propionate.mechanism_of_action: Corticosteroids bind to glucocorticoid receptors, altering gene expression to reduce inflammation. They decrease capillary permeability, leukocyte migration, and inhibit key inflammatory mediators like phospholipase A2 and NF-Kappa B. At lower doses, they are anti-inflammatory, while higher doses suppress the immune system. Prolonged high doses can also activate mineralocorticoid receptors, affecting sodium and potassium balance.
Clobetasol propionate.pharmacodynamics: Corticosteroids bind to glucocorticoid receptors, suppressing pro-inflammatory pathways and enhancing anti-inflammatory effects. Clobetasol propionate, a potent corticosteroid, is typically applied twice daily for prolonged action. These drugs have a wide therapeutic window, allowing for higher dosing than endogenous levels. However, patients should be warned about risks of HPA axis suppression and increased infection susceptibility.

Cytarabine.name: Cytarabine
Cytarabine.description: Cytarabine is a pyrimidine nucleoside analog used primarily to treat leukemia, especially acute non-lymphoblastic leukemia. It acts as an antimetabolite antineoplastic agent by inhibiting DNA synthesis. Its effects are specific to the S phase of the cell cycle. Additionally, cytarabine has antiviral and immunosuppressant properties.
Cytarabine.mechanism_of_action: Cytarabine is a cytotoxic agent that primarily targets proliferating cells during DNA synthesis (S-phase). It inhibits DNA polymerase, disrupting DNA replication and repair. Cytarabine is incorporated into DNA and RNA, causing direct DNA damage and further impairing cellular function. This leads to cell cycle arrest and apoptosis in rapidly dividing cells.
Cytarabine.pharmacodynamics: Cytarabine is an antimetabolite chemotherapy agent primarily used to treat acute myelogenous and meningeal leukemia. It is converted intracellularly to its active triphosphate form, which interferes with DNA synthesis during the S phase of the cell cycle. The drug inhibits DNA polymerases and incorporates into DNA, disrupting normal cell division and repair. Its cytotoxic effects are highly specific to rapidly dividing cells, leading to effective leukemia cell death.

Dabrafenib.name: Dabrafenib
Dabrafenib.description: Dabrafenib mesylate (Tafinlar) is a kinase inhibitor that targets the MAPK pathway. It is mainly indicated for treating melanoma and metastatic non-small cell lung cancer with BRAF V600E or V600K mutations. In combination with trametinib (Mekinist), it is also approved for anaplastic thyroid cancer with the BRAF V600E mutation. Key effects include inhibition of tumor growth in cancers driven by these mutations.
Dabrafenib.mechanism_of_action: Dabrafenib is a selective inhibitor of the BRAF kinase, binding competitively to its ATP pocket. It has higher affinity for mutant BRAF forms, especially BRAF V600E, V600K, and V600D. By inhibiting BRAF, dabrafenib blocks the MAPK pathway, which regulates cell proliferation and survival. This action is particularly effective in cancers with constitutively active BRAF mutations.
Dabrafenib.pharmacodynamics: Dabrafenib is a kinase inhibitor targeting BRAF V600E mutations, often used in combination with trametinib to inhibit different points in the RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK pathway. This combination increases response rates and reduces resistance without added toxicity. In melanoma, the COMBI-AD Phase III trial showed improved relapse-free survival with dabrafenib plus trametinib after surgery. The regimen also demonstrates potent, well-tolerated clinical activity in BRAF V600E–mutated anaplastic thyroid cancer, offering a significant therapeutic advance.

Dapagliflozin.name: Dapagliflozin
Dapagliflozin.description: Dapagliflozin is an SGLT2 inhibitor used primarily to manage type 2 diabetes. It improves glycemic control by blocking glucose reabsorption in the kidneys, leading to increased glucose excretion. The drug can be used alone or with other diabetes medications. It is also approved to reduce the risk of kidney decline, heart failure, and cardiovascular death in adults with chronic kidney disease.
Dapagliflozin.mechanism_of_action: Dapagliflozin inhibits the sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) in the proximal renal tubule. This reduces glucose reabsorption in the kidneys, leading to increased urinary glucose excretion. The result is improved glycemic control and potential weight loss in patients with type 2 diabetes.
Dapagliflozin.pharmacodynamics: Dapagliflozin reduces sodium reabsorption and increases sodium delivery to the distal tubule, lowering cardiac pre- and afterload and decreasing intraglomerular pressure. It promotes urinary glucose excretion, leading to increased urinary volume, with effects returning to baseline within days after discontinuation. In clinical studies, dapagliflozin did not cause clinically significant QTc prolongation, even at doses much higher than recommended. These pharmacodynamic actions contribute to improved glycemic control and potential cardiovascular and renal benefits.

Daptomycin.name: Daptomycin
Daptomycin.description: Daptomycin is a cyclic lipopeptide antibiotic active against Gram-positive bacteria, including MRSA, MSSA, and VRE. It is mainly indicated for serious infections such as complicated skin and soft tissue infections and bacteremia. Key effects include rapid bactericidal activity with a low risk of resistance. Once-daily dosing reduces side effects, particularly myopathy, while maintaining efficacy.
Daptomycin.mechanism_of_action: Daptomycin acts by binding to bacterial membranes in a calcium-dependent manner, primarily targeting phosphatidylglycerol (PG)-rich domains. This binding disrupts membrane function, leading to loss of membrane potential and inhibition of essential processes such as nutrient transport and ATP synthesis. Additionally, daptomycin may interfere with cell wall biosynthetic enzymes and cell division by altering membrane fluidity and forming complexes with cell envelope precursors. The precise downstream effects remain unclear, but disruption of the bacterial membrane is central to its bactericidal activity.
Daptomycin.pharmacodynamics: Daptomycin is a cyclic lipopeptide antibiotic with rapid, concentration-dependent bactericidal activity against a broad range of aerobic Gram-positive bacteria, including MRSA and VRE. It is ineffective for treating pneumonia due to inhibition by lung surfactant. Key adverse effects include myopathy, rhabdomyolysis, hypersensitivity reactions, eosinophilic pneumonia, and possible peripheral neuropathy, requiring regular CPK and renal function monitoring. Daptomycin should not be used in patients under one year of age, and its use may affect coagulation tests and promote resistance if not used appropriately.

Darolutamide.name: Darolutamide
Darolutamide.description: Darolutamide is a nonsteroidal androgen receptor antagonist used to treat non-metastatic castrate-resistant prostate cancer (nmCRPC). It is indicated for patients whose prostate cancer has progressed despite prior androgen receptor antagonist therapy. The drug works by delaying progression to metastatic disease, aiming to improve quality of life and life expectancy. Darolutamide was developed by Bayer and approved by the FDA in July 2019.
Darolutamide.mechanism_of_action: Darolutamide is an androgen receptor (AR) antagonist that competitively inhibits androgens from binding to AR, blocking AR nuclear translocation and AR-mediated transcription. This leads to reduced prostate cancer cell proliferation and tumor size. Its main metabolite, keto-darolutamide, has similar activity. Darolutamide binds more tightly to AR than other antagonists like apalutamide and enzalutamide, and has minimal progesterone receptor antagonism of unclear clinical relevance.
Darolutamide.pharmacodynamics: Darolutamide is a potent androgen receptor antagonist used to treat castrate-resistant prostate cancer. It inhibits cancer cell growth by blocking androgen receptor signaling. This leads to a marked reduction in prostate specific antigen (PSA) levels. The overall effect is slowed disease progression and improved clinical outcomes.

Delamanid.name: Delamanid
Delamanid.description: Delamanid is an oral anti-tuberculosis drug from the nitro-dihydro-imidazooxazole class that inhibits mycolic acid synthesis in bacterial cell walls. It is mainly indicated for the treatment of multidrug-resistant and extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis as part of a combination regimen. Clinical studies show delamanid improves treatment outcomes and reduces mortality when used with optimized background therapy. Spontaneous resistance can occur due to mutations affecting drug bioactivation, and the drug is marketed as Deltyba.
Delamanid.mechanism_of_action: Delamanid is a prodrug activated by the mycobacterial F420 coenzyme system, particularly the nitroreductase Rv3547. Once activated, it inhibits methoxy-mycolic and keto-mycolic acid synthesis, disrupting mycobacterial cell wall formation. This leads to cell death and is effective against both growing and nongrowing mycobacteria. Resistance can occur through mutations in F420 coenzyme-related genes, and delamanid does not affect alpha-mycolic acid synthesis.
Delamanid.pharmacodynamics: Delamanid exhibits potent activity against _Mycobacterium tuberculosis_ with MIC values between 0.006 and 0.024 g/mL, and also shows _in vitro_ activity against _M. kansasii_ and _M. bovis_. It is ineffective against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria, and does not display cross-resistance with other anti-tuberculosis drugs. In animal models, delamanid reduces _M. tuberculosis_ colony counts in a dose-dependent manner. Clinically, repeated dosing may cause QTc prolongation and affect blood clotting by increasing PT and APTT.

Dexmedetomidine.name: Dexmedetomidine
Dexmedetomidine.description: This drug is an adrenergic alpha-2 receptor agonist used in veterinary medicine. Its main indications are for providing analgesia and sedation in animals. It is the racemic form of dexmedetomidine. Key effects include pain relief and calming or sedative action.
Dexmedetomidine.mechanism_of_action: Dexmedetomidine is a selective alpha-2 adrenoceptor agonist. It binds to presynaptic alpha-2 receptors, inhibiting norepinephrine release and reducing pain signal transmission. Activation of postsynaptic alpha-2 receptors decreases sympathetic activity, leading to lower blood pressure and heart rate.
Dexmedetomidine.pharmacodynamics: Dexmedetomidine activates α2-adrenoceptors, leading to reduced sympathetic tone. This results in decreased neuroendocrine and hemodynamic responses during anesthesia and surgery. It provides sedation and analgesia, while also lowering anesthetic and opioid requirements. These effects contribute to improved perioperative management.

Digitoxin.name: Digitoxin
Digitoxin.description: This drug is a cardiac glycoside, occasionally used as an alternative to digoxin. It is primarily indicated for heart conditions such as heart failure and certain arrhythmias. The drug has a longer half-life than digoxin, resulting in prolonged effects. Toxicity is similar to digoxin but lasts longer due to its extended duration.
Digitoxin.mechanism_of_action: Digitoxin inhibits the Na-K-ATPase pump, leading to increased intracellular sodium and calcium levels. The elevated calcium enhances activation of cardiac contractile proteins, improving heart muscle contraction. Additionally, digitoxin alters cardiac electrical activity by increasing phase 4 depolarization slope, shortening action potential duration, and lowering maximal diastolic potential. These effects contribute to its therapeutic action in heart conditions.
Digitoxin.pharmacodynamics: Digitoxin is a cardiac glycoside with a longer half-life and duration of toxic effects compared to digoxin. It is eliminated by the liver, making it suitable for patients with impaired kidney function. However, digitoxin is rarely used today and lacks strong clinical evidence for heart failure management. Its toxic effects are similar to those of digoxin but persist longer.

Dimethyl fumarate.name: Dimethyl fumarate
Dimethyl fumarate.description: Dimethyl fumarate is an oral medication primarily indicated for the treatment of relapsing forms of multiple sclerosis. Its exact mechanism is unclear, but it is believed to work by activating the Nrf2 pathway through its active metabolite, monomethyl fumarate, helping to reduce oxidative stress. The drug is marketed as Tecfidera and was the third oral disease-modifying therapy for multiple sclerosis approved by the FDA. Before its approval for MS, dimethyl fumarate was used in Germany to treat psoriasis.
Dimethyl fumarate.mechanism_of_action: Dimethyl fumarate is metabolized to monomethyl fumarate (MMF), its active form. Both compounds activate the Nrf2 pathway, enhancing cellular resistance to oxidative stress. Dimethyl fumarate also inhibits nuclear factor kappa B, reducing pro-inflammatory gene expression. These actions lead to immunomodulation by shifting lymphocyte populations from pro-inflammatory to anti-inflammatory phenotypes and reducing CNS immune cell infiltration.
Dimethyl fumarate.pharmacodynamics: Dimethyl fumarate exerts anti-inflammatory and cytoprotective effects, contributing to its benefit in multiple sclerosis treatment. It does not significantly prolong the QT interval. However, serious adverse effects such as progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy, opportunistic infections, lymphopenia, and liver injury have been reported. Rarely, it may also cause anaphylaxis and angioedema.

Diphenhydramine.name: Diphenhydramine
Diphenhydramine.description: Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) is a first-generation H1 antihistamine used mainly to treat allergies, insect bites, and rashes. It also has antiemetic, antitussive, hypnotic, and antiparkinson effects. Its central nervous system activity often causes sedation, making it a common ingredient in over-the-counter sleep aids and nighttime cold medications. Additionally, it is being studied for potential anxiolytic and antidepressant properties due to its effects on various neurotransmitter systems.
Diphenhydramine.mechanism_of_action: Diphenhydramine acts primarily as an inverse agonist at H1 histamine receptors, blocking histamine's effects and reducing allergy symptoms. It readily crosses the blood-brain barrier, antagonizing central H1 receptors to cause drowsiness and suppress coughing. Additionally, it functions as a competitive antagonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors, contributing anticholinergic and antiparkinson effects. Diphenhydramine also blocks intracellular sodium channels, which can provide local anesthetic properties.
Diphenhydramine.pharmacodynamics: Diphenhydramine acts primarily as an H1-receptor antagonist, blocking histamine effects such as congestion and spasms in various tissues. It also provides anti-emetic effects by inhibiting the medullary chemoreceptor trigger zone. Its anti-vertigo action is due to central antimuscarinic effects on the vestibular system and vomiting center. Clinically, these actions result in relief from allergy symptoms, nausea, vertigo, and sedation.

Encorafenib.name: Encorafenib
Encorafenib.description: Encorafenib (BRAFTOVI) is a kinase inhibitor that targets the BRAF gene, specifically inhibiting the B-raf protein involved in cell signaling pathways. It is mainly indicated for the treatment of unresectable or metastatic melanoma with BRAF V600E or V600K mutations, in combination with binimetinib. The drug disrupts abnormal cell growth and division associated with these mutations. It was FDA-approved for this use in June 2018.
Encorafenib.mechanism_of_action: Encorafenib is a kinase inhibitor primarily targeting mutant BRAF V600E, as well as wild-type BRAF and CRAF. By inhibiting these kinases, it disrupts abnormal signaling that promotes tumor cell growth. Encorafenib also binds to other kinases, such as JNKs, LIMKs, MEK4, and STK36, at clinically relevant concentrations. Its main therapeutic effect is blocking the MAPK pathway in cancer cells with BRAF mutations.
Encorafenib.pharmacodynamics: Encorafenib is a BRAF inhibitor with a distinct pharmacologic and tolerability profile, demonstrating improved efficacy in metastatic melanoma, including both BRAFi-pretreated and naïve patients. It effectively inhibits tumor growth in cell lines and animal models with BRAF V600E, D, and K mutations by suppressing the RAF/MEK/ERK pathway. Combination therapy with encorafenib and binimetinib enhances anti-tumor activity and delays resistance compared to monotherapy in BRAF-mutant melanoma and NSCLC models. In BRAF-mutant colorectal cancer, combining encorafenib with EGFR inhibitors like cetuximab overcomes resistance and provides superior anti-tumor effects.

Erdafitinib.name: Erdafitinib
Erdafitinib.description: Balversa (erdafitinib) is an oral FGFR kinase inhibitor approved for treating locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma with susceptible FGFR3 or FGFR2 genetic alterations. It is indicated for patients whose cancer has progressed during or after platinum-containing chemotherapy. The drug represents the first personalized therapy targeting FGFR mutations in metastatic bladder cancer. Key effects include providing a new, targeted treatment option for a common and difficult-to-treat cancer.
Erdafitinib.mechanism_of_action: Erdafitinib is an oral, selective inhibitor of fibroblast growth factor receptors (FGFR1-4). It works by binding to and inhibiting the kinase activity of these FGFRs, blocking their phosphorylation and downstream signaling. This inhibition reduces cell proliferation and survival in cancer cells with FGFR genetic alterations. As a result, erdafitinib shows antitumor activity, particularly in cancers like bladder cancer with FGFR pathway dysregulation.
Erdafitinib.pharmacodynamics: Erdafitinib increases serum phosphate levels due to FGFR inhibition, and dosing is adjusted to maintain target phosphate levels of 5.5–7.0 mg/dL. During clinical trials, drugs and supplements that could further raise phosphate were generally prohibited, and phosphate binders were used to manage elevations. Agents affecting phosphate levels were avoided before dose adjustments. Erdafitinib did not cause significant QTc interval prolongation in clinical studies.

Everolimus.name: Everolimus
Everolimus.description: Everolimus is an mTOR inhibitor derived from Rapamycin (sirolimus). It is primarily used as an immunosuppressant to prevent organ transplant rejection. Everolimus specifically targets the mTORC1 protein, without affecting mTORC2. Its main effect is to suppress immune responses by inhibiting cell growth and proliferation.
Everolimus.mechanism_of_action: Everolimus binds to FKBP-12, forming a complex that inhibits the mTOR pathway. This inhibition blocks cell cycle progression from G1 to S phase, causing cell growth arrest and apoptosis. Everolimus also reduces hypoxia-inducible factor and vascular endothelial growth factor expression, limiting angiogenesis. Overall, it decreases cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and glucose uptake.

Fenofibrate.name: Fenofibrate
Fenofibrate.description: Fenofibrate is a fibric acid derivative used to manage lipid disorders. It is indicated for primary hypercholesterolemia, mixed dyslipidemia, and severe hypertriglyceridemia. The drug works by lowering cholesterol and triglyceride levels in the blood. Fenofibrate received FDA approval in December 1993.
Fenofibrate.mechanism_of_action: Fenofibrate activates the nuclear receptor PPARα, which regulates genes involved in lipid metabolism. This leads to increased lipolysis and activation of lipoprotein lipase, while reducing apoprotein C-III levels. The drug enhances peroxisome formation, generating reactive oxygen species that further promote lipid breakdown. Increased oxidative stress from this process can rarely cause liver damage, such as cirrhosis or chronic hepatitis.
Fenofibrate.pharmacodynamics: Fenofibrate activates PPARα to modify lipid metabolism, effectively treating hypercholesterolemia, mixed dyslipidemia, and severe hypertriglyceridemia. It is administered once daily due to its long half-life of 19-27 hours. The drug has a wide therapeutic index with typical doses ranging from 50-150 mg daily. Patients should be warned about potential risks such as rhabdomyolysis, myopathy, and cholelithiasis.

Flutamide.name: Flutamide
Flutamide.description: An antiandrogen with about the same potency as cyproterone in rodent and canine species.
Flutamide.mechanism_of_action: Flutamide is a nonsteroidal antiandrogen that blocks androgen receptors, preventing testosterone from exerting its effects. It inhibits testosterone-stimulated DNA synthesis in prostate cells. Additionally, flutamide reduces the nuclear uptake of androgens in prostatic tissue. These actions collectively suppress androgen-driven prostate growth and activity.
Flutamide.pharmacodynamics: Flutamide is a nonsteroidal antiandrogen that inhibits androgen uptake and nuclear binding in target tissues. This action leads to potent antiandrogenic effects, particularly beneficial in treating androgen-sensitive prostatic carcinoma. Treatment with flutamide counteracts androgen effects, similar to castration. Administration of flutamide is associated with increased plasma testosterone and estradiol levels.

Fulvestrant.name: Fulvestrant
Fulvestrant.description: Fulvestrant is an estrogen receptor antagonist used to treat hormone receptor (HR)-positive metastatic breast cancer in post-menopausal women. It is indicated for patients with disease progression after anti-estrogen therapy. The drug works by down-regulating and degrading estrogen receptors, with no agonist effects. Fulvestrant can be used alone or in combination with alpelisib for HR-positive, HER2-negative, PIK3CA-mutated advanced or metastatic breast cancer.
Fulvestrant.mechanism_of_action: Fulvestrant competitively binds to estrogen receptors in cancer cells. It downregulates these receptors, preventing estrogen from binding. Additionally, fulvestrant promotes degradation of the bound estrogen receptors. These actions inhibit the growth of both tamoxifen-resistant and estrogen-sensitive breast cancer cells.
Fulvestrant.pharmacodynamics: Fulvestrant for intramuscular administration is an estrogen receptor antagonist without known agonist effects.

Fusidic acid.name: Fusidic acid
Fusidic acid.description: This drug is an antibiotic derived from Fusidium coccineum fermentation. It is mainly indicated for bacterial infections and works by inhibiting translocation during protein synthesis. The drug is commonly used topically in creams and eyedrops, but systemic forms like tablets and injections are also available. Key effects include the inhibition of bacterial growth and infection control.
Fusidic acid.mechanism_of_action: Fusidic acid inhibits bacterial protein synthesis by blocking the translocation of elongation factor G (EF-G) on the ribosome. This prevents proper movement of the ribosome during translation. Additionally, fusidic acid can inhibit chloramphenicol acetyltransferase enzymes. Its main targets are EF-G and these acetyltransferase enzymes.
Fusidic acid.pharmacodynamics: Fusidic acid is a bacteriostatic antibiotic and helps prevent bacterial growth while the immune system clears the infection.

Gefitinib.name: Gefitinib
Gefitinib.description: Gefitinib is an anti-cancer drug marketed as Iressa by AstraZeneca. It is primarily indicated for the treatment of certain types of non-small cell lung cancer. Gefitinib works by selectively targeting mutant proteins in malignant cells, inhibiting their growth. Its mechanism is similar to that of erlotinib (Tarceva).
Gefitinib.mechanism_of_action: Gefitinib selectively inhibits the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) tyrosine kinase by binding to its ATP-binding site. EGFR is often overexpressed in certain cancers, leading to increased cell survival and proliferation. By blocking EGFR activation, gefitinib disrupts downstream signaling pathways that promote tumor growth. This results in reduced malignant cell proliferation.
Gefitinib.pharmacodynamics: Gefitinib blocks intracellular phosphorylation of tyrosine kinases, particularly those linked to the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR). By inhibiting EGFR-TK, it disrupts signaling pathways involved in cell growth and survival. This action is especially relevant in cancer cells that overexpress EGFR. Clinically, gefitinib can slow tumor progression in EGFR-positive cancers.

Gemfibrozil.name: Gemfibrozil
Gemfibrozil.description: Gemfibrozil is a fibric acid derivative used to treat certain types of hyperlipidemia (Types IIb, IV, and V). It is prescribed for patients who have not responded to lifestyle changes or other medications. The drug helps lower lipid levels in the blood. Gemfibrozil received FDA approval in December 1981.
Gemfibrozil.mechanism_of_action: Gemfibrozil activates PPARα, leading to changes in lipid metabolism. This results in increased HDL and enhanced activity of lipoprotein lipase, which lowers plasma triglyceride levels. It also inhibits apoB synthesis and increases its clearance, reducing VLDL production. Additionally, its glucuronide metabolite inhibits the CYP2C8 enzyme.
Gemfibrozil.pharmacodynamics: Gemfibrozil modifies lipid metabolism and is used to treat hyperlipidemia. It requires twice-daily dosing due to a mean residence time of up to 9.6 hours in patients with chronic renal failure. The drug has a wide therapeutic index, with double doses not causing severe side effects in trials. However, it may increase the risk of cholelithiasis and cholecystitis.

Glasdegib.name: Glasdegib
Glasdegib.description: Glasdegib is a small-molecule hedgehog signaling inhibitor used primarily to treat acute myeloid leukemia (AML). It works by blocking the smoothened (SMO) receptor, disrupting cancer cell growth driven by aberrant hedgehog pathway activation. Glasdegib is most effective when combined with low-dose cytarabine, improving survival and response rates in AML patients. Its favorable side effect profile has prompted ongoing research into new combination therapies for older AML patients.
Glasdegib.mechanism_of_action: Glasdegib is a selective inhibitor of the hedgehog signaling pathway. It works by binding to and inhibiting the smoothened (SMO) receptor, a key component in this pathway. By blocking SMO, glasdegib prevents downstream activation of transcription factors and gene expression involved in cell proliferation. This inhibition targets cancers with aberrant hedgehog pathway activation, such as chronic myeloid leukemia and basal cell carcinoma.
Glasdegib.pharmacodynamics: Glasdegib significantly reduced leukemic stem cell burden and marker expression in preclinical xenograft models. In clinical trials, it downregulated GL11 expression by over 80% and led to complete remission in 8% and stable disease in 31% of acute myeloid leukemia patients. The drug improved overall survival to 8.3 months, nearly doubling survival compared to low-dose cytarabine. Dose-dependent QTc prolongation has also been reported as a safety concern.

Idoxuridine.name: Idoxuridine
Idoxuridine.description: An analog of deoxyuridine that inhibits viral DNA synthesis. The drug is used as an antiviral agent.
Idoxuridine.mechanism_of_action: Idoxuridine is an antiviral agent that incorporates into viral DNA by substituting for thymidine. This substitution disrupts the function of viral DNA polymerases and inhibits thymidylate phosphorylase. As a result, viral DNA replication is impaired. Ultimately, this prevents the virus from reproducing and infecting tissue.
Idoxuridine.pharmacodynamics: Idoxuridine mimics thymidine, allowing it to be incorporated into viral DNA during replication. This leads to the formation of defective viral DNA, rendering the Herpes simplex virus non-infective and unable to damage tissue. Its antiviral effect is most pronounced during active DNA synthesis in infected cells, which occurs early in lesion development. Continuous application is recommended to ensure the affected area remains exposed to the drug during this critical period.

Infigratinib.name: Infigratinib
Infigratinib.description: Infigratinib is a pan-fibroblast growth factor receptor (FGFR) kinase inhibitor. It is primarily indicated for adults with previously treated, unresectable locally advanced or metastatic cholangiocarcinoma that has an FGFR2 fusion or rearrangement. The drug works by inhibiting the FGFR pathway, thereby suppressing tumor growth. Infigratinib received FDA accelerated approval in 2021 under the brand name Truseltiq.
Infigratinib.mechanism_of_action: Fibroblast growth factor receptors (FGFRs) are tyrosine kinases involved in cell growth and survival, with aberrant FGFR signalling linked to various cancers. Infigratinib is a reversible, non-competitive inhibitor targeting all four FGFR subtypes, with highest affinity for FGFR1, FGFR2, and FGFR3. It binds to the ATP-binding cleft of FGFRs, preventing receptor autophosphorylation. This blocks downstream signalling, including the MAPK pathway, thereby inhibiting cancer cell proliferation.
Infigratinib.pharmacodynamics: Infigratinib is an anti-tumor agent that targets FGFR2 and FGFR3 alterations, suppressing tumor growth in cholangiocarcinoma. It demonstrated anti-tumor activity in animal models and showed a 23% overall response rate in clinical trials, including one complete response. The median duration of response was 5.5 months, with a wide range observed. However, some patients with FGFR mutations exhibit intrinsic resistance, limiting efficacy and prompting ongoing research into overcoming drug resistance.

Ipatasertib.name: Ipatasertib
Ipatasertib.description: Ipatasertib has been used in trials studying the treatment of Cancer, Neoplasms, Solid Cancers, Breast Cancer, and Gastric Cancer, among others.

LB-100.name: LB-100
LB-100.description: LB-100 is an investigational drug currently being studied for the treatment of low or intermediate-1 risk myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS). Its main indication is to address blood cell abnormalities associated with MDS. LB-100 is designed to modulate cellular processes that may improve bone marrow function. Key effects are being evaluated in ongoing clinical trials to determine its safety and efficacy.

Larotrectinib.name: Larotrectinib
Larotrectinib.description: Larotrectinib is an oral inhibitor of tropomyosin receptor kinase (Trk), a protein involved in cancer cell growth and survival. It is primarily indicated for the treatment of solid tumors that are Trk-positive, regardless of tumor location (tissue-agnostic). The drug works by blocking Trk activation, leading to tumor cell death and inhibition of tumor growth. Larotrectinib received accelerated FDA approval in 2018 and full approval in 2025.
Larotrectinib.mechanism_of_action: Larotrectinib is a selective inhibitor of tropomyosin receptor kinases TRKA, TRKB, and TRKC. These kinases, encoded by NTRK genes, regulate neuronal growth and survival, but gene fusions or mutations can lead to constitutively active TRK proteins that drive tumor proliferation. Larotrectinib blocks the activity of these aberrant TRK fusion proteins, demonstrating anti-tumor effects in models with TRK activation. Its efficacy is reduced in tumors with specific resistance-associated point mutations in TRKA and TRKC kinase domains.
Larotrectinib.pharmacodynamics: Larotrectinib potently inhibits TRKA, TRKB, and TRKC enzymes with IC50 values of 5-11 nM, and shows much weaker inhibition of TNK2. It does not cause clinically relevant QTc interval prolongation, even at doses nine times higher than recommended. No dose adjustment is needed for renal impairment, but dose reductions are required for moderate to severe hepatic impairment. These effects support its targeted use and safety profile in diverse patient populations.

Lipoic acid.name: Lipoic acid
Lipoic acid.mechanism_of_action: Lipoic acid acts as a cofactor in oxidative decarboxylation of keto acids, supporting cellular energy metabolism. The biologically active R-enantiomer facilitates antioxidant effects and metabolic processes. The S-enantiomer can inhibit the activity of the R-enantiomer, potentially increasing oxidative stress. Additionally, the S-enantiomer reduces GLUT-4 expression, leading to decreased glucose uptake and insulin sensitivity.
Lipoic acid.pharmacodynamics: Lipoic acid crosses the blood-brain barrier and mobilizes mercury from brain cells into the bloodstream, where other chelators like DMSA can facilitate its excretion. This combination is hypothesized to aid in treating conditions linked to mercury toxicity, such as autism and amalgam poisoning. Lipoic acid also supports cellular glucose uptake and is used in diabetes management. Additionally, it may offer benefits for neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's.

Lonafarnib.name: Lonafarnib
Lonafarnib.description: Lonafarnib (ZOKINVY™) is a farnesyl transferase inhibitor used to treat Hutchinson-Gilford progeria syndrome (HGPS) and related progeroid laminopathies. It works by reducing the farnesylation of progerin, thereby decreasing its accumulation in cells and improving disease symptoms. Lonafarnib is the first FDA-approved therapy for HGPS, offering a targeted approach to this rare, fatal premature aging disorder. The drug is marketed by Eiger Biopharmaceuticals.
Lonafarnib.mechanism_of_action: Lonafarnib is a selective inhibitor of farnesyl transferase (FTase), blocking the farnesylation of prelamin A and its mutant form, progerin. By preventing farnesylation, lonafarnib reduces the accumulation of progerin at the inner nuclear membrane. This action slows the progression of Hutchinson-Gilford progeria syndrome (HGPS) and related laminopathies. The drug specifically targets FTase without significantly affecting the related GGPT-1 enzyme.
Lonafarnib.pharmacodynamics: Lonafarnib inhibits farnesyl transferase, reducing farnesylation of proteins such as progerin, which is implicated in Hutchinson-Gilford progeria syndrome. Clinically, it may cause electrolyte abnormalities, myelosuppression, and elevated liver enzymes, though direct causation is uncertain. Animal studies indicate risks of nephrotoxicity, vision decline, fertility impairment, and embryo-fetal toxicity at plasma levels similar to human dosing. Regular monitoring of renal, ophthalmologic, and reproductive function is recommended during treatment.

Lucanthone.name: Lucanthone
Lucanthone.description: This drug is a schistosomicide, previously used to treat schistosomiasis. Its use has largely been replaced by hycanthone and praziquantel. Currently, it is being investigated as a radiation sensitizer. Key effects include targeting parasitic infections and potentially enhancing radiation therapy.
Lucanthone.mechanism_of_action: Lucanthone inhibits DNA repair in tumor cells following radiation exposure. It targets and inhibits AP endonuclease and topoisomerase II enzymes. This disruption prevents effective repair of radiation-induced DNA damage. As a result, lucanthone enhances the cytotoxic effects of radiation in tumor cells.
Lucanthone.pharmacodynamics: Lucanthone is structurally similar to Actinomycin D but lacks hematological and gastrointestinal toxicity at clinically tolerated doses. Clinical trials have shown lucanthone to be safe, practical, and effective for cancer treatment. Its specificity for brain tumors is due to preferential action on cycling cells and efficient crossing of the blood-brain barrier. These properties support its use in combination with radiation for treating brain tumors.

Malotilate.name: Malotilate
Malotilate.description: Malotilate is a small molecule drug. Malotilate has a monoisotopic molecular weight of 288.05 Da.

Medroxyprogesterone acetate.name: Medroxyprogesterone acetate
Medroxyprogesterone acetate.description: Medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA) is a synthetic progesterone derivative with enhanced metabolic stability. It is primarily indicated for treating secondary amenorrhea, endometrial hyperplasia, abnormal uterine bleeding, and as a contraceptive. Additional uses include managing menopausal symptoms, endometriosis pain, and as palliative therapy for endometrial and renal cancers. MPA was approved by the FDA in 1959.
Medroxyprogesterone acetate.mechanism_of_action: Medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA) primarily inhibits gonadotropin production, preventing follicular maturation and ovulation. It thins the endometrium and reduces nuclear estrogen receptors and DNA synthesis in endometrial epithelial cells. Additionally, MPA can induce p53-dependent apoptosis in certain cancer cell lines. It also inhibits GABA-A receptors.
Medroxyprogesterone acetate.pharmacodynamics: Medroxyprogesterone acetate (MPA) inhibits gonadotropin production, decreases endometrial estrogen receptor activity, and induces apoptosis in cancer cells. It has a long duration of action due to a half-life of 40–60 hours (oral) and even longer with injectable forms. MPA has a wide therapeutic window, with doses ranging from 5 mg orally to 1000 mg by injection weekly. Long-term use can reduce bone density, increasing the risk of osteoporosis and fractures, especially in adolescents.

Neratinib.name: Neratinib
Neratinib.description: Neratinib (Nerlynx) is an oral tyrosine kinase inhibitor approved for extended adjuvant therapy in HER2-positive breast cancer. It helps reduce the risk of cancer recurrence after initial treatment. The drug is manufactured by Puma Biotechnology Inc. Neratinib is also being studied for potential use in other types of cancer.
Neratinib.mechanism_of_action: Neratinib irreversibly inhibits the EGFR, HER2, and HER4 receptors. This blocks autophosphorylation of tyrosine residues on these receptors. As a result, downstream oncogenic signaling via the MAPK and Akt pathways is reduced. This inhibition helps limit cancer cell growth and survival.
Neratinib.pharmacodynamics: Neratinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting EGFR, HER2, and HER4 receptors. It blocks signaling pathways involved in tumor cell growth and proliferation. This results in antitumor effects against cancers that overexpress these receptors. Clinically, neratinib is used to treat HER2-positive carcinomas.

Oleic acid.name: Oleic Acid
Oleic acid.description: Oleic acid is an unsaturated fatty acid commonly found in nature. It is mainly used in the production of oleates, lotions, and as a pharmaceutical solvent. Its key effects include acting as an emollient and carrier in topical and oral formulations. Oleic acid helps enhance drug absorption and stability in pharmaceutical products.

PH-797804.name: PH-797804
PH-797804.description: PH-797804 has been investigated for the treatment of Osteoarthritis.

Panobinostat.name: Panobinostat
Panobinostat.description: Panobinostat (brand name Farydak) is an oral, non-selective histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor. It is primarily indicated for the treatment of multiple myeloma. The drug was FDA-approved based on improved progression-free survival, with ongoing confirmatory trials to further establish its efficacy. Panobinostat is considered the most potent HDAC inhibitor currently available.
Panobinostat.mechanism_of_action: Panobinostat is a histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor that targets multiple HDAC classes (I, II, and IV). By inhibiting HDACs, it disrupts the acetylation of proteins, leading to altered gene expression and impaired protein metabolism. This results in antitumor effects, particularly in multiple myeloma where HDACs are overexpressed. Panobinostat also works synergistically with bortezomib to enhance cytotoxicity against cancer cells.

Pentagastrin.name: Pentagastrin
Pentagastrin.description: This drug is a synthetic pentapeptide that mimics endogenous gastrin. It is mainly indicated for stimulating gastric acid, pepsin, and intrinsic factor secretion. Its key effects include increased gastric secretions. Additionally, it is used as a diagnostic aid in certain gastrointestinal conditions.
Pentagastrin.mechanism_of_action: Pentagastrin is a synthetic analogue of natural gastrin that primarily stimulates oxyntic cells in the stomach to secrete gastric acid, pepsin, and intrinsic factor. It also promotes pancreatic secretion, especially at high doses. Additionally, pentagastrin increases gastrointestinal motility by acting directly on intestinal smooth muscle. It delays gastric emptying by stimulating terminal antral contractions, leading to enhanced retropulsion.
Pentagastrin.pharmacodynamics: Pentagastrin stimulates gastric acid secretion and is used diagnostically to assess gastric acid secretory function. It helps identify anacidity (achlorhydria) in conditions like pernicious anemia, atrophic gastritis, or gastric carcinoma. The drug is also useful for evaluating changes in acid output following peptic ulcer surgeries, such as vagotomy or gastric resection. Clinical outcomes focus on confirming impaired or altered gastric acid production.

Pentoxifylline.name: Pentoxifylline
Pentoxifylline.description: Pentoxifylline is a synthetic dimethylxanthine derivative that improves blood flow and has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects. It is mainly used to treat intermittent claudication in patients with peripheral arterial disease. The drug has also been explored for conditions involving fibrosis, diabetic kidney disease, and COVID-19-related pulmonary complications. Pentoxifylline has been available in various branded and generic forms since the 1980s.
Pentoxifylline.mechanism_of_action: Pentoxifylline primarily lowers blood viscosity by increasing erythrocyte flexibility and reducing plasma fibrinogen, while also inhibiting neutrophil activation and platelet aggregation. Its main mechanism involves elevating intracellular cAMP levels, either through non-specific phosphodiesterase inhibition or by enhancing adenosine A2A receptor signaling. Increased cAMP activates protein kinase A (PKA), which suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokine production and reduces oxidative stress. Additionally, PTX disrupts TGF-β1 signaling, leading to anti-fibrotic effects and further improvement in blood flow.
Pentoxifylline.pharmacodynamics: Pentoxifylline improves blood flow by reducing blood viscosity and has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects. It is mainly used to treat peripheral arterial disease but may cause angina, arrhythmias, and hypotension in patients with additional cardiovascular conditions. When combined with warfarin, closer monitoring of prothrombin times is recommended. Patients with bleeding risks should be regularly monitored for signs of hemorrhage.

Pimitespib.name: Pimitespib
Pimitespib.description: TAS-116 is under investigation in clinical trial NCT02965885 (A Study of TAS-116 in Patients With Solid Tumors).

Pimozide.name: Pimozide
Pimozide.description: This drug is a diphenylbutylpiperidine antipsychotic. It is mainly indicated for treating psychotic disorders and for suppressing vocal and motor tics in Tourette syndrome, serving as an alternative to haloperidol.
