Its key effect is believed to involve blocking postsynaptic dopamine receptors, though the exact mechanism is not fully understood.
Pimozide.mechanism_of_action: Pimozide suppresses motor and phonic tics in Tourette's Disorder mainly by blocking dopaminergic activity. It binds to and inhibits dopamine D2 receptors in the central nervous system. This antagonism reduces excessive dopamine signaling, which is associated with tic symptoms. The drug’s therapeutic effect is directly linked to its action on dopamine pathways.
Pimozide.pharmacodynamics: Pimozide is an oral antipsychotic that primarily blocks dopamine receptors in the central nervous system. This blockade leads to changes in dopamine metabolism, contributing to both its therapeutic effects and potential side effects. Pimozide also affects other CNS receptor systems, though these actions are not fully understood. Compared to other antipsychotics, it causes less sedation and hypotension due to its more selective dopamine receptor activity, making it a suitable alternative to haloperidol.

Pioglitazone.name: Pioglitazone
Pioglitazone.description: Pioglitazone is an oral antihyperglycemic drug from the thiazolidinedione class used to manage type 2 diabetes mellitus, typically alongside diet, exercise, and other antidiabetic agents. Its main effect is to improve insulin sensitivity and enhance glucose uptake by activating the PPARγ receptor. While effective, its use has declined due to concerns over serious adverse effects such as congestive heart failure and bladder cancer. Safer and more effective alternatives are now preferred for most patients.
Pioglitazone.mechanism_of_action: Pioglitazone is a selective agonist of the peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor-gamma (PPARγ) in insulin-responsive tissues. By activating PPARγ, it increases transcription of genes that regulate glucose and lipid metabolism. This enhances tissue sensitivity to insulin and reduces hepatic glucose production. As a result, pioglitazone improves insulin resistance in type 2 diabetes without increasing insulin secretion.
Pioglitazone.pharmacodynamics: Pioglitazone increases cellular sensitivity to insulin, improving glucose disposal and overall glucose homeostasis. In type 2 diabetes patients, this leads to reduced plasma glucose, insulin levels, and HbA1c. However, it can cause significant fluid retention and may worsen or trigger congestive heart failure, so it should be avoided in at-risk patients. There is also a potential increased risk of bladder cancer, requiring caution in patients with current or past bladder cancer.

Ponatinib.name: Ponatinib
Ponatinib.description: Ponatinib is a Bcr-Abl tyrosine kinase inhibitor used to treat chronic myeloid leukemia (CML). It is particularly effective against CML with the T315I mutation. The drug was FDA approved on December 14, 2012. Key effects include inhibition of abnormal cell growth in leukemia.
Ponatinib.mechanism_of_action: Ponatinib is a multi-target kinase inhibitor primarily acting on the Bcr-Abl tyrosine kinase, including the T315I mutant form associated with resistant chronic myeloid leukemia (CML). It blocks the kinase activity of Bcr-Abl, preventing cell proliferation driven by the Philadelphia chromosome. Ponatinib also inhibits other kinases such as VEGFR, PDGFR, FGFR, EPH receptors, SRC family, KIT, RET, TIE2, and FLT3. This broad inhibition leads to reduced tumor growth in models expressing both native and mutant Bcr-Abl.

Posaconazole.name: Posaconazole
Posaconazole.description: Posaconazole is a triazole antifungal medication. It is primarily used to treat invasive infections caused by Candida and Aspergillus species. The drug is indicated for severely immunocompromised patients. Its key effect is the inhibition of fungal growth, helping to control serious fungal infections.
Posaconazole.mechanism_of_action: Posaconazole is a triazole antifungal that inhibits the fungal enzyme sterol 14α-demethylase, a cytochrome P-450 dependent enzyme. By binding to the enzyme's heme cofactor, it blocks ergosterol synthesis, an essential component of the fungal cell membrane. This disruption leads to accumulation of toxic sterol precursors. The result is impaired fungal cell growth and cell death.
Posaconazole.pharmacodynamics: Posaconazole is an antifungal agent structurally related to itraconazole, with chemical modifications that improve its potency and broaden its spectrum of activity. It acts by inhibiting fungal growth and can be either fungicidal or fungistatic depending on the organism and concentration. Clinically, these properties make posaconazole effective against a wide range of fungal infections. Its enhanced activity offers improved outcomes in the treatment and prevention of invasive fungal diseases.

Quinestrol.name: Quinestrol
Quinestrol.mechanism_of_action: Estrogens diffuse into target cells and bind to intracellular estrogen receptors. The estrogen-receptor complex enters the nucleus and regulates gene transcription, leading to specific protein synthesis. Estrogens increase hepatic production of serum proteins like SHBG and TBG, and suppress FSH release from the anterior pituitary. In combination with progestins, estrogens further suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, reducing GnRH secretion.
Quinestrol.pharmacodynamics: Quinestrol is a synthetic estrogen that acts as a long-acting prodrug of ethinyl estradiol. After oral absorption, it is stored in adipose tissue and gradually released, providing sustained estrogenic effects. Its main clinical outcome is prolonged estrogen activity, useful in hormone therapy. The active metabolite, ethinyl estradiol, mimics natural estradiol’s effects.

Radotinib.name: Radotinib
Radotinib.description: Radotinib is under investigation for the treatment of Leukemia, Myelogenous, Chronic, BCR-ABL Positive.
Radotinib.mechanism_of_action: Philadelphia chromosome-positive leukemia is caused by the abnormal BCR-ABL1 fusion kinase, which has constant tyrosine kinase activity. This activity promotes uncontrolled cell growth. Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) specifically block BCR-ABL1 function. TKIs are effective treatments for chronic myeloid leukemia and Ph+ acute lymphoblastic leukemia.

Raltitrexed.name: Raltitrexed
Raltitrexed.description: Raltitrexed (Tomudex®) is an antimetabolite chemotherapy drug produced by AstraZeneca. It is primarily indicated for the treatment of advanced colorectal cancer. The drug works by inhibiting thymidylate synthase, an enzyme essential for DNA synthesis. This action prevents cancer cell growth and proliferation.
Raltitrexed.mechanism_of_action: Raltitrexed is a folic acid antagonist that inhibits thymidylate synthase, an enzyme essential for DNA synthesis. It enters cells via the reduced folate carrier and is then polyglutamated, increasing its potency and duration of action. By blocking thymidylate synthase, raltitrexed reduces thymidine triphosphate levels, leading to impaired DNA synthesis and cell death.
Raltitrexed.pharmacodynamics: Raltitrexed is an antimetabolite used primarily to treat colon and rectal cancer. It works by blocking a key enzyme required for cell survival, inhibiting cancer cell growth and leading to their destruction. Normal cells may also be affected, resulting in side effects that can sometimes be serious. Common adverse effects include hair loss and other symptoms that should be reported to a doctor.

Regorafenib.name: Regorafenib
Regorafenib.description: Regorafenib is an oral multi-kinase inhibitor. It is mainly indicated for metastatic colorectal cancer, advanced gastrointestinal stromal tumors, and hepatocellular carcinoma. The drug was FDA approved in 2012, with expanded approval for hepatocellular carcinoma in 2017. Key effects include inhibition of tumor growth and progression.
Regorafenib.mechanism_of_action: Regorafenib is a small molecule inhibitor targeting multiple protein kinases involved in cancer cell growth, angiogenesis, and the tumor microenvironment. Its main targets include VEGFR1-3, KIT, PDGFR-α/β, FGFR1/2, RET, and RAF kinases. By inhibiting these kinases, regorafenib disrupts tumor blood vessel formation and cancer cell proliferation. In preclinical models, it has shown anti-angiogenic, anti-tumor, and anti-metastatic effects.

Resveratrol.name: Resveratrol
Resveratrol.description: Resveratrol is a polyphenolic compound found in plants, classified as a stilbenoid. It is mainly researched for its antioxidant and potential anti-inflammatory effects. Key indications include cardiovascular health, metabolic support, and possible anti-aging benefits. The trans-isomer is more stable under normal conditions, though resveratrol can degrade when exposed to light, heat, or oxygen.
Resveratrol.mechanism_of_action: Resveratrol inhibits the activation of NF-kappaB in cells infected with HSV. HSV normally activates NF-kappaB to promote its replication. By suppressing NF-kappaB, resveratrol interferes with the viral replication process. The main target of resveratrol in this mechanism is the NF-kappaB signaling pathway.
Resveratrol.pharmacodynamics: Resveratrol inhibits replication of herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2 in a dose-dependent, reversible manner. It also exhibits anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects. Epidemiological data suggest an association between higher resveratrol intake and lower heart disease incidence. Preclinical studies indicate potential anticancer properties.

Rimonabant.name: Rimonabant
Rimonabant.description: Rimonabant is an anti-obesity drug developed by Sanofi-Aventis. It acts as an inverse agonist at the cannabinoid CB1 receptor, primarily reducing appetite. The drug is approved for weight loss in several countries, including the EU, Mexico, and Brazil. However, it was not approved in the United States due to concerns about increased risk of depression and suicidal thoughts.
Rimonabant.mechanism_of_action: Rimonabant is a selective antagonist of the CB1 cannabinoid receptor. By blocking CB1 receptors, it inhibits the activity of the endocannabinoid system. This leads to reduced appetitive drive and behaviors associated with excessive endocannabinoid signaling. Its mechanism suggests therapeutic potential for conditions like obesity and substance abuse.
Rimonabant.pharmacodynamics: In the RIO-North America trial, patients receiving 20 mg rimonabant experienced significant weight loss and reduced waist circumference. They also showed improved insulin sensitivity and increased HDL cholesterol levels compared to placebo. These effects indicate beneficial metabolic outcomes with rimonabant 20 mg. Lower dose (5 mg) results were not highlighted for clinical significance.

Ritonavir.name: Ritonavir
Ritonavir.description: Ritonavir is an HIV protease inhibitor primarily used as a pharmacokinetic booster to enhance the effectiveness of other protease inhibitors. Its main indications include HIV treatment and, in combination therapies, the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection and COVID-19. Ritonavir increases drug levels by inhibiting CYP3A, thereby improving the efficacy of co-administered antiviral agents. It is available in various fixed-dose combination products for HCV and COVID-19, and requires careful management in patients co-infected with HIV to prevent drug resistance.
Ritonavir.mechanism_of_action: Ritonavir inhibits the HIV protease enzyme, preventing cleavage of the gag-pol polyprotein and resulting in immature, noninfectious viral particles. It also potently inhibits the cytochrome P450 CYP3A4 isoenzyme by irreversibly binding to its active site, affecting drug metabolism. Additionally, ritonavir may limit cellular transport and efflux of other protease inhibitors via P-glycoprotein and MRP channels. These actions enhance antiviral efficacy and increase plasma levels of co-administered drugs.
Ritonavir.pharmacodynamics: Ritonavir is a protease inhibitor that blocks HIV-1 protease, preventing the cleavage of viral polyproteins and leading to the production of immature, non-infectious HIV particles. Its main clinical effect is to reduce viral replication and improve immune function in patients with HIV-1. Ritonavir is commonly used in combination with other antiretroviral drugs for enhanced efficacy. Additionally, low-dose ritonavir boosts the pharmacokinetic exposure of other protease inhibitors by inhibiting their metabolism via the CYP3A4 enzyme pathway.

Sapanisertib.name: Sapanisertib
Sapanisertib.description: Sapanisertib has been used in trials studying the treatment of HCC, Solid Tumor, Gliosarcoma, Liver Cancer, and Glioblastoma, among others.

Sulfisoxazole.name: Sulfisoxazole
Sulfisoxazole.description: A short-acting sulfonamide antibacterial with activity against a wide range of gram- negative and gram-positive organisms.
Sulfisoxazole.mechanism_of_action: Sulfisoxazole competitively inhibits the enzyme dihydropteroate synthetase in bacteria. It blocks the condensation of pteridine with para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), preventing dihydrofolic acid formation. This inhibition disrupts bacterial folic acid synthesis, which is essential for their growth and replication.
Sulfisoxazole.pharmacodynamics: Sulfisoxazole is a bacteriostatic sulfonamide antibiotic effective against many gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, though resistance is common. It inhibits bacterial growth by competitively blocking p-aminobenzoic acid in folic acid metabolism. Sulfonamides are well-absorbed orally and widely distributed in body tissues and fluids, but their efficacy is reduced in the presence of pus. Resistance to one sulfonamide generally means resistance to all in the class.

TAK-733.name: TAK-733
TAK-733.description: TAK-733 is an investigational drug studied in clinical trials. Its main indications include advanced metastatic melanoma and other advanced nonhematologic malignancies. The drug is being evaluated for its potential anti-cancer effects in these conditions. Key effects are still under investigation as part of ongoing research.

TAK-901.name: TAK-901
TAK-901.description: TAK-901 is an investigational drug studied primarily for cancer treatment. Its main indications include lymphoma, myelofibrosis, multiple myeloma, myeloid metaplasia, and advanced solid tumors. TAK-901 is being evaluated for its potential to inhibit tumor growth and improve outcomes in these conditions. Key effects are still under investigation in clinical trials.

Talc.name: Talc
Talc.description: Talc is a mineral commonly used in cosmetic and personal hygiene products to absorb moisture and reduce friction, helping prevent rashes. Its main indications are for use in powders such as baby powder and body powder. Key effects include moisture absorption and skin protection. However, recent studies suggest talc use may increase the risk of ovarian and endometrial cancers by about 30%.
Talc.mechanism_of_action: It has very good absorptive properties.

Temsirolimus.name: Temsirolimus
Temsirolimus.description: Temsirolimus is a derivative of sirolimus marketed as Torisel. It is primarily indicated for the treatment of renal cell carcinoma (RCC). The drug works by inhibiting mTOR, leading to reduced tumor cell growth and proliferation. Temsirolimus received FDA and EMA approval in 2007.
Temsirolimus.mechanism_of_action: Temsirolimus inhibits the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) by forming a complex with FKBP-12, blocking mTOR’s kinase activity. This inhibition leads to cell cycle arrest at the G1 phase in tumor cells. It prevents phosphorylation of downstream targets like p70S6k and S6 ribosomal protein in the PI3K/AKT pathway. Additionally, temsirolimus reduces levels of HIF-1α, HIF-2α, and VEGF, limiting tumor growth and angiogenesis.

Tirabrutinib.name: Tirabrutinib
Tirabrutinib.description: Tirabrutinib is an investigational drug being studied for its safety and pharmacokinetics. It is primarily being evaluated in healthy volunteers and patients with rheumatoid arthritis (RA). The main indication is RA, aiming to assess its potential therapeutic effects. Key effects under investigation include its safety profile and how the body processes the drug.

Tolcapone.name: Tolcapone
Tolcapone.description: Tolcapone is a COMT inhibitor used as an adjunct therapy for Parkinson's disease alongside levodopa/carbidopa. It helps enhance and prolong the effects of levodopa by reducing its breakdown. The drug is a yellow, odorless, crystalline compound. Tolcapone carries a significant risk of hepatotoxicity.
Tolcapone.mechanism_of_action: Tolcapone inhibits the enzyme catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT), reducing peripheral metabolism of levodopa. This increases plasma levodopa concentrations and decreases levels of its metabolite, 3-OMD. Lower 3-OMD allows more levodopa to enter the central nervous system. The result is more consistent dopaminergic stimulation, improving symptoms of Parkinson’s disease.
Tolcapone.pharmacodynamics: Tolcapone is a potent, selective, and reversible inhibitor of COMT, an enzyme involved in the breakdown of catechols like dopamine and levodopa. By inhibiting COMT, tolcapone increases and sustains plasma levels of levodopa when used with a decarboxylase inhibitor. This leads to more consistent dopaminergic stimulation in the brain, improving Parkinson’s disease symptoms. However, it may also increase levodopa-related adverse effects, sometimes requiring dose adjustment.

Tomivosertib.name: Tomivosertib
Tomivosertib.description: Tomivosertib is under investigation in clinical trial NCT03318562 (A PD Study of Oral eFT508 in Subjects With Advanced TNBC and HCC).

Trametinib.name: Trametinib
Trametinib.description: Trametinib is an oral inhibitor of MEK1 and MEK2 enzymes. It is mainly indicated for the treatment of cancers with BRAF mutations, such as melanoma, non-small cell lung cancer, and thyroid cancer. The drug can be used alone or in combination with dabrafenib for enhanced effect. Its key action is blocking cancer cell growth by inhibiting the MEK pathway.
Trametinib.mechanism_of_action: The MAPK/ERK pathway regulates cell proliferation, survival, and growth, with BRAF mutations causing constitutive activation in many melanomas. Trametinib is a selective inhibitor of MEK1 and MEK2, blocking their catalytic activity and preventing pathway activation. Dabrafenib inhibits mutant BRAF, and combined use with trametinib synergistically suppresses tumor growth and delays resistance in BRAF V600 mutation-positive cancers. This dual inhibition targets key steps in the pathway for improved therapeutic efficacy.
Trametinib.pharmacodynamics: Trametinib inhibits the growth of BRAF V600 mutation-positive tumors both in vitro and in vivo. It is commonly combined with dabrafenib, a BRAF inhibitor, to enhance therapeutic effects. In BRAF-mutant colorectal cancer, resistance to BRAF inhibitors can occur due to EGFR-mediated MAPK pathway re-activation. This limits the clinical efficacy of BRAF-targeted therapies in these patients.

Triamcinolone.name: Triamcinolone
Triamcinolone.description: Triamcinolone is a corticosteroid used to treat various inflammatory conditions, including allergic rhinitis, multiple sclerosis exacerbations, and corticosteroid-responsive skin disorders. It is also used as an adjunct for osteoarthritic knee pain and, in its acetonide form, for macular edema associated with uveitis via suprachoroidal injection. The drug reduces inflammation and immune responses in affected tissues. Triamcinolone was first approved by the FDA in 1957.
Triamcinolone.mechanism_of_action: Triamcinolone, a corticosteroid, inhibits phospholipase A2, blocking the release of arachidonic acid from cell membranes. This reduces cyclooxygenase and lipoxygenase activity, lowering prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis. It also reverses vascular dilation and permeability, limiting immune cell migration. Additionally, triamcinolone suppresses nuclear factor kappa-B, decreasing pro-inflammatory cytokine production.
Triamcinolone.pharmacodynamics: Triamcinolone is a corticosteroid with strong anti-inflammatory effects. It is used to reduce inflammation in various organs and tissues. Clinically, it helps manage symptoms in multiple inflammatory conditions. However, triamcinolone should not be given as an epidural injection.

Triclosan.name: Triclosan
Triclosan.description: This drug is an aromatic ether known as triclosan. It is mainly used as a preservative and antimicrobial agent. Its primary indications are in personal care products like soaps, creams, toothpaste, and deodorants, as well as in household items. Triclosan helps reduce or prevent bacterial contamination.
Triclosan.mechanism_of_action: Triclosan primarily inhibits bacterial growth by targeting fatty acid synthesis. It binds to the enoyl-acyl carrier protein reductase (ENR) enzyme, forming a ternary complex with NAD+. This complex blocks fatty acid synthesis, weakening the bacterial cell membrane and leading to cell death. Humans are not affected because they lack the ENR enzyme.

Trifluridine.name: Trifluridine
Trifluridine.description: Trifluridine is a fluorinated pyrimidine nucleoside with antiviral and anticancer properties. As an ophthalmic solution, it is mainly used to treat primary keratoconjunctivitis and recurrent epithelial keratitis caused by herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2. In combination with tipiracil (as Lonsurf), it is approved for metastatic colorectal cancer in adults who have failed standard therapies. Its key effect in cancer treatment is disrupting DNA function in cancer cells, inhibiting their replication.
Trifluridine.mechanism_of_action: Trifluridine inhibits viral and tumor cell replication by incorporating into DNA during synthesis, leading to defective DNA and impaired cell function. In cancer cells, it is phosphorylated to its active form and disrupts DNA synthesis and tumor cell proliferation. Tipiracil is combined with trifluridine to inhibit thymidine phosphorylase, increasing trifluridine’s bioavailability. Trifluridine also weakly inhibits thymidylate synthetase, but this effect is not the main contributor to its anticancer activity.
Trifluridine.pharmacodynamics: Trifluridine exhibits antiviral activity against herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2, vaccinia virus, and some adenovirus strains, and is effective in treating herpetic keratitis, including cases resistant to idoxuridine or vidarabine. In combination with tipiracil, trifluridine demonstrates antitumor effects in both 5-FU sensitive and resistant colorectal cancer models, primarily through incorporation into cancer cell DNA. Clinical studies show that trifluridine/tipiracil improves progression-free survival, overall response rate, and disease control rate in metastatic colorectal cancer patients. Trifluridine at recommended dosages does not cause clinically significant QT/QTc prolongation, though rare cases of notable QTc increases have been observed.

Trimetrexate.name: Trimetrexate
Trimetrexate.description: This drug is a nonclassical folic acid inhibitor that blocks dihydrofolate reductase. It is mainly being tested as an antineoplastic (anticancer) and antiparasitic agent, particularly for pneumocystis pneumonia in AIDS patients. Its key effect is the inhibition of cell growth. Myelosuppression is the primary dose-limiting toxicity.
Trimetrexate.mechanism_of_action: Trimetrexate acts as a competitive inhibitor of dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR) across various organisms. By blocking DHFR, it prevents the conversion of dihydrofolate to tetrahydrofolate, an essential coenzyme. This depletion interferes with thymidylate and folate-dependent biosynthesis pathways. Ultimately, it disrupts DNA, RNA, and protein synthesis, leading to cell death.
Trimetrexate.pharmacodynamics: Trimetrexate is a synthetic inhibitor of dihydrofolate reductase, blocking the reduction of folic acid needed for DNA synthesis and cell reproduction. It primarily affects rapidly dividing cells, such as malignant cells and cells of the skin, mucosa, bone marrow, and fetal tissues. This selective action helps impair tumor growth while minimizing irreversible damage to normal tissues. Due to its serious and potentially life-threatening side effects, leucovorin rescue therapy is required for at least 72 hours after treatment.

Tucatinib.name: Tucatinib
Tucatinib.description: Tucatinib is a kinase inhibitor used in combination with trastuzumab and capecitabine. It is indicated for the treatment of unresectable or metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer. The drug is intended for patients who have not responded to previous chemotherapy. Tucatinib can improve outcomes in this difficult-to-treat patient group.
Tucatinib.mechanism_of_action: Tucatinib is a selective inhibitor of the HER-2 tyrosine kinase enzyme. By blocking HER-2 activity, it prevents phosphorylation of both HER-2 and HER-3, disrupting downstream MAPK and AKT signaling pathways. This inhibition reduces cell proliferation in HER-2 expressing tumor cells. Tucatinib’s anti-tumor effects are primarily observed in cancers with HER-2 mutations.
Tucatinib.pharmacodynamics: Tucatinib inhibits tyrosine kinase, leading to anti-tumor effects in HER2-positive breast cancer. It reduces tumor size by targeting HER2 signaling pathways. Clinical trials show that combining tucatinib with trastuzumab enhances efficacy compared to either drug alone. This combination results in improved clinical outcomes for patients with HER2-positive breast cancer.

Vemurafenib.name: Vemurafenib
Vemurafenib.description: Vemurafenib is a competitive kinase inhibitor targeting mutant BRAF, especially the V600E mutation. It is mainly indicated for the treatment of unresectable or metastatic melanoma with BRAF V600E mutation. The drug works by binding to the ATP-binding domain of the mutant BRAF kinase, inhibiting its activity. Key effects include blocking tumor cell proliferation driven by the mutated BRAF pathway.
Vemurafenib.mechanism_of_action: Vemurafenib is an oral inhibitor targeting mutated BRAF-serine-threonine kinase, specifically the BRAF V600E mutation. It acts as a competitive inhibitor, blocking the kinase's activity. This inhibition disrupts downstream signaling pathways, suppressing tumor growth and inducing apoptosis. Vemurafenib is ineffective against cells with wild-type BRAF.
Vemurafenib.pharmacodynamics: BRAF activation promotes cell growth, proliferation, and metastasis via the MAPK pathway. The V600E mutation leads to constant BRAF activity, driving uncontrolled cellular proliferation. Vemurafenib inhibits BRAF, reducing phosphorylated ERK, Ki-67-driven proliferation, and MAPK-related metabolic activity. Clinical studies show vemurafenib achieves near-complete inhibition of the MAPK pathway.

Volasertib.name: Volasertib
Volasertib.description: Volasertib is an investigational anti-cancer drug that inhibits Polo-like kinase 1 (PLK1). It has been studied primarily for the treatment of various leukemias, including acute myeloid leukemia (AML) and acute monocytic leukemia, as well as myelodysplastic syndromes and other neoplasms. The drug works by disrupting cell division, leading to cancer cell death. Key effects include inhibition of tumor growth and potential improvement in blood cell counts.

Vortioxetine.name: Vortioxetine
Vortioxetine.description: Vortioxetine is an antidepressant used to treat major depressive disorder (MDD). It is classified as a serotonin modulator and stimulator (SMS) due to its multimodal action on the serotonin system. Vortioxetine works by inhibiting serotonin reuptake and modulating several serotonin receptors. Its key effects include improving mood and alleviating depressive symptoms.
Vortioxetine.mechanism_of_action: Vortioxetine is a serotonin modulator and stimulator with a multimodal mechanism of action. It inhibits the serotonin transporter, increasing serotonin levels in the synaptic cleft. Additionally, it acts as an agonist at 5-HT1A receptors, partial agonist at 5-HT1B, and antagonist at 5-HT3, 5-HT1D, and 5-HT7 receptors. These combined actions enhance serotonergic neurotransmission and contribute to its antidepressant effects.
Vortioxetine.pharmacodynamics: Vortioxetine selectively inhibits serotonin reuptake by binding with high affinity to the serotonin transporter, leading to increased serotonin levels. It does not significantly affect norepinephrine or dopamine transporters. Vortioxetine also interacts with multiple serotonin receptors, acting as an antagonist at 5-HT3, 5-HT1D, and 5-HT7, a partial agonist at 5-HT1B, and an agonist at 5-HT1A. These combined actions enhance serotonergic neurotransmission and contribute to its antidepressant effects.

Zileuton.name: Zileuton
Zileuton.description: Zileuton is a selective 5-lipoxygenase inhibitor used to manage asthma. It works by blocking the formation of leukotrienes, substances that cause airway inflammation, bronchoconstriction, and mucus production. By inhibiting leukotriene synthesis, Zileuton reduces asthma symptoms such as inflammation and bronchospasm. The immediate-release tablet form of Zileuton has been withdrawn from the US market.
Zileuton.mechanism_of_action: Zileuton is a selective inhibitor of 5-lipoxygenase, the enzyme responsible for converting arachidonic acid into leukotrienes. By blocking this enzyme, zileuton reduces the formation of leukotrienes such as LTB4, LTC4, LTD4, and LTE4. This inhibition decreases inflammation, bronchoconstriction, and other airway symptoms associated with asthma. Both R(+) and S(-) enantiomers of zileuton are active in inhibiting 5-lipoxygenase.
Zileuton.pharmacodynamics: Zileuton is an asthma medication that inhibits 5-lipoxygenase, blocking leukotriene synthesis. By reducing leukotriene production, it decreases airway inflammation and bronchoconstriction associated with asthma. Clinically, zileuton has been shown to attenuate bronchoconstriction in asthmatic patients, such as during cold air challenges. This mechanism distinguishes it from other antiasthmatic agents.

olaparib.name: Olaparib
olaparib.description: Olaparib is a selective inhibitor of PARP1 and PARP2 enzymes, used as an anti-cancer therapy. It is indicated for treating BRCA-associated tumors, including ovarian, breast, pancreatic, and prostate cancers. The drug works by exploiting defective DNA repair in cancer cells with BRCA mutations, leading to cell death. Olaparib was first approved in 2014 by the FDA and EU, and in 2016 by Health Canada.
olaparib.mechanism_of_action: Olaparib is a selective inhibitor of PARP1 and PARP2, enzymes involved in DNA repair, particularly the base excision repair pathway. By inhibiting PARP activity, olaparib prevents repair of single-strand DNA breaks, leading to accumulation of double-strand breaks. In cells with BRCA1/2 mutations, which lack effective homologous recombination repair, this results in genomic instability and cell death. This targeted killing of BRCA-mutant cancer cells is known as synthetic lethality.
olaparib.pharmacodynamics: Olaparib is a cytotoxic agent that inhibits tumour growth in vitro and in mouse models, both alone and after platinum-based chemotherapy. It is particularly effective in cancers with BRCA1/2, ATM, or other homologous recombination repair (HRR) gene deficiencies. Olaparib enhances the effects of DNA-damaging chemotherapeutic agents and radiotherapy by acting as a chemosensitizer and radiosensitizer. These actions result in increased tumour cell death and improved anti-tumour outcomes in preclinical studies.

palbociclib.name: Palbociclib
palbociclib.description: Palbociclib is a second-generation cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor used in cancer therapy. Its main indication is for the treatment of hormone receptor (HR)-positive, HER2-negative advanced or metastatic breast cancer in both women and men. The drug works by interfering with cell cycle machinery, inhibiting cancer cell growth. It was developed by Pfizer and received FDA approval in 2015, with expanded indications in 2019.
palbociclib.mechanism_of_action: Palbociclib selectively inhibits cyclin-dependent kinases 4 and 6 (CDK4/6) by binding to their ATP pocket. This blocks the CDK4/6-cyclin D complex, preventing phosphorylation of retinoblastoma protein. As a result, E2F transcription factors remain inactive, halting the G1-S cell cycle transition. This inhibition restricts cell proliferation in tumors dependent on this pathway.
palbociclib.pharmacodynamics: Palbociclib inhibits cell growth by blocking DNA replication and inducing cell-cycle arrest in RB-proficient cancer cells, primarily increasing the proportion of cells in the G1 phase. It effectively reduces proliferation and promotes senescence in estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer cell lines, especially those with high _RB1_ and _CCND1_ expression and low _CDKN2A_. When combined with antiestrogens, palbociclib enhances antitumor activity in preclinical models. Clinically, palbociclib plus letrozole significantly improves progression-free survival and response rates in metastatic breast cancer patients.

Captopril.name: Captopril
Captopril.description: Captopril is a potent, competitive inhibitor of angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE). It is primarily indicated for the treatment of hypertension. By blocking ACE, captopril reduces the formation of angiotensin II, leading to lower blood pressure. Its key effect is the modulation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS).
Captopril.mechanism_of_action: ACE exists in two isoforms: somatic (with N and C domains) and testicular, with the C-domain mainly regulating blood pressure. ACE inhibitors, like captopril, bind both domains but have much higher affinity for the C-domain. Captopril competitively inhibits ACE, blocking conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, thereby lowering blood pressure. This inhibition also increases plasma renin activity due to reduced angiotensin II feedback.
Captopril.pharmacodynamics: Captopril is an ACE inhibitor that blocks the conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, thereby antagonizing the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS). This results in reduced aldosterone and vasopressin secretion, decreased sodium and water reabsorption, and direct arterial vasodilation, all leading to lower blood pressure. Additionally, captopril increases bradykinin levels, further promoting vasodilation. Clinically, these effects help manage hypertension and heart failure.

Cilostazol.name: Cilostazol
Cilostazol.description: Cilostazol is an antiplatelet drug with vasodilating effects, classified as a quinolinone derivative. It is primarily used to treat intermittent claudication in patients with peripheral ischemia. The drug works by inhibiting platelet aggregation and reducing calcium-induced muscle contractions. It is marketed under the brand name Pletal.
Cilostazol.mechanism_of_action: Cilostazol is a selective phosphodiesterase III (PDE III) inhibitor. It blocks the breakdown of cyclic AMP (cAMP) in platelets and blood vessels. This increases cAMP levels, resulting in reduced platelet aggregation and vasodilation. The overall effect is improved blood flow and decreased clot formation.
Cilostazol.pharmacodynamics: Cilostazol improves symptoms of intermittent claudication by increasing walking distance. It alleviates leg pain that occurs during walking and resolves with rest. The drug works by enhancing blood flow to the legs. Clinically, this leads to better mobility and reduced discomfort for affected patients.

Tucidinostat.name: Tucidinostat
Tucidinostat.mechanism_of_action: Chidamide is an oral histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor from the benzamide class. It blocks HDAC enzymes, leading to increased acetylation of histones. This results in altered gene expression, promoting cancer cell cycle arrest and apoptosis. Its main target is the selective regulation of gene expression in cancer cells.

crizotinib.name: Crizotinib
crizotinib.description: Crizotinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor used to treat ALK or ROS1-positive non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), ALK-positive anaplastic large cell lymphoma (ALCL), and inflammatory myofibroblastic tumor (IMT). It works by targeting specific genetic rearrangements, such as the EML4-ALK fusion protein, to inhibit tumor growth. Crizotinib was the first drug approved for ALK-positive tumors and received FDA approval in 2011. Its effectiveness is confirmed by FDA-approved tests for ALK and ROS1 rearrangements, though newer inhibitors have addressed some resistance issues.
crizotinib.mechanism_of_action: Crizotinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor that primarily targets ALK, ROS1, and c-MET receptors. It blocks the phosphorylation and activation of these kinases, particularly inhibiting oncogenic ALK fusion proteins found in some non-small cell lung cancers. This inhibition disrupts signaling pathways that promote cell proliferation and survival. As a result, crizotinib reduces tumor growth and viability in cancers driven by these genetic alterations.
crizotinib.pharmacodynamics: Crizotinib demonstrated clinical efficacy in ALK-positive non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), with a 6-month progression-free survival rate of 72% and improved two-year overall survival compared to untreated patients. Key pharmacodynamic effects include inhibition of tumor growth in ALK mutation-positive cancers. However, crizotinib use is associated with serious adverse effects such as hepatotoxicity, interstitial lung disease, QT prolongation, bradycardia, and severe visual loss. These risks are particularly notable in pediatric and young adult patients with ALCL or IMT.

Mifepristone.name: Mifepristone
Mifepristone.description: Mifepristone is a hormone antagonist that blocks progestational and glucocorticoid receptors. It is primarily used to induce bleeding for medical termination of early pregnancy and to treat hypercortisolism in nonpituitary Cushing syndrome. The drug is available as Mifeprex® (200mg) and Korlym™ (300mg). Mifepristone is also being studied for potential use in psychotic depression.
Mifepristone.mechanism_of_action: Mifepristone acts as a competitive antagonist at progesterone receptors, blocking progesterone activity. This inhibition leads to termination of pregnancy by preventing progesterone from maintaining the uterine lining. In Cushing's syndrome, mifepristone blocks cortisol receptors, reducing the effects of excess cortisol without lowering its production. The main targets are progesterone and glucocorticoid receptors.
Mifepristone.pharmacodynamics: Mifepristone is a synthetic steroid that blocks progesterone, leading to termination of early intrauterine pregnancy. It increases myometrial sensitivity to prostaglandins, promoting uterine contractions. The drug also has antiglucocorticoid effects, causing increased ACTH and cortisol levels in humans. Weak antiandrogenic activity is present but is not clinically significant.

Hexylresorcinol.name: Hexylresorcinol
Hexylresorcinol.description: Hexylresorcinol is a substituted dihydroxybenzene compound with antiseptic, anthelmintic, and local anesthetic properties. It is mainly used topically for minor skin infections and in oral solutions or lozenges for pain relief and as a first aid antiseptic. Additionally, it is found in some cosmetic anti-aging creams. Research is ongoing regarding its potential use as an anti-cancer therapy.
Hexylresorcinol.mechanism_of_action: Hexylresorcinol acts as an oral anesthetic by blocking voltage-gated neuronal sodium channels, inhibiting nerve impulse transmission and reducing pain locally. As an antiseptic, it disrupts bacterial biofilm formation, cell chain formation, and adherence, while inhibiting glycolytic enzymes and altering cell surface properties. It also exhibits anti-inflammatory and anti-aging effects by inhibiting NF-kappaB phosphorylation and tyrosinase activity, increasing glutathione synthesis, and promoting skin lightening. Additionally, hexylresorcinol induces apoptosis and differentiation in squamous cell carcinoma cells by modulating E2F signaling and inhibiting transglutaminase-2 activity.
Hexylresorcinol.pharmacodynamics: Hexylresorcinol acts as a local anesthetic on the mouth and throat mucous membranes by blocking sodium channels. It also exhibits mild antiseptic properties. Additionally, hexylresorcinol has anti-inflammatory and demulcent effects. These actions contribute to symptom relief in topical applications.

Econazole.name: Econazole
Econazole.description: A broad spectrum antimycotic with some action against Gram positive bacteria. It is used topically in dermatomycoses also orally and parenterally.
Econazole.mechanism_of_action: Econazole primarily inhibits 14-α demethylase, a cytochrome P-450 enzyme involved in converting lanosterol to ergosterol. This blocks ergosterol synthesis, disrupting fungal cell membrane integrity and increasing permeability. Econazole may also affect respiration, membrane phospholipids, yeast-to-mycelial transformation, purine uptake, and lipid biosynthesis. These combined actions impair fungal cell survival and growth.
Econazole.pharmacodynamics: Econazole is an antifungal medication that inhibits the production of essential substances needed for fungal growth and function. It is related to other azole antifungals like fluconazole and ketoconazole. Clinically, econazole is effective only against fungal infections and has no activity against bacteria or viruses.

Ribociclib.name: Ribociclib
Ribociclib.description: Ribociclib (brand name Kisqali) is a selective cyclin-dependent kinase (CDK4/6) inhibitor. It is primarily indicated for the treatment of certain types of breast cancer. The drug works by blocking CDK4/6 proteins, slowing cancer cell growth and division. Ribociclib was approved by the FDA in March 2017.
Ribociclib.mechanism_of_action: Ribociclib selectively inhibits cyclin-dependent kinases 4 and 6 (CDK4/6). These kinases, when activated by D-cyclins, promote cell cycle progression by phosphorylating the retinoblastoma protein (pRb). Ribociclib blocks pRb phosphorylation, causing cell cycle arrest in the G1 phase and reducing cellular proliferation. This mechanism leads to decreased tumor cell growth, particularly in breast cancer models.
Ribociclib.pharmacodynamics: Ribociclib induces concentration-dependent prolongation of the QTcF interval. This effect increases the risk of cardiac arrhythmias, such as Torsades de Pointes (TdP). Caution is advised when used with other QTc-prolonging drugs or in patients with conditions like long QT syndrome. Monitoring of cardiac function is recommended during treatment.

Terfenadine.name: Terfenadine
Terfenadine.description: In the U.S., Terfenadine was superseded by fexofenadine in the 1990s due to the risk of cardiac arrhythmia caused by QT interval prolongation.
Terfenadine.mechanism_of_action: Terfenadine acts as a competitive antagonist at histamine H1-receptors in the GI tract, uterus, blood vessels, and bronchial muscle. By reversibly blocking these receptors, it reduces histamine-induced symptoms such as edema, flare, and pruritus. Terfenadine does not significantly cross the blood-brain barrier, resulting in minimal central nervous system depression.
Terfenadine.pharmacodynamics: Terfenadine is an H1-receptor antagonist antihistamine that blocks histamine-mediated allergic responses. Its structure is similar to astemizole and haloperidol. Terfenadine is metabolized into fexofenadine, which is responsible for its clinical antihistamine effects. The drug is used to relieve symptoms of allergic conditions such as rhinitis and urticaria.

Ornidazole.name: Ornidazole
Ornidazole.description: Ornidazole has been used in trials studying the prevention of Elective Colorectal Surgery.

Rifaximin.name: Rifaximin
Rifaximin.description: Rifaximin is a non-systemic, rifamycin-based antibiotic that acts locally in the gastrointestinal tract. It is primarily indicated for treating traveler's diarrhea caused by E. coli, reducing the risk of recurrent hepatic encephalopathy, and managing diarrhea-predominant irritable bowel syndrome (IBS-D) in adults. The drug does not enter the bloodstream, minimizing systemic effects. It is marketed under the brand name Xifaxan by Salix Pharmaceuticals.
Rifaximin.mechanism_of_action: Rifaximin inhibits bacterial RNA synthesis by binding to the beta-subunit of DNA-dependent RNA polymerase. This action blocks the translocation step during transcription. As a result, bacterial RNA production is halted, preventing bacterial growth. The drug’s core target is the bacterial RNA polymerase enzyme.
Rifaximin.pharmacodynamics: Rifaximin is a non-absorbable, gut-specific antibiotic that inhibits bacterial RNA synthesis, helping to restore intestinal microflora balance. Its non-systemic action is due to its unique chemical structure. Rifaximin also activates the pregnane X receptor (PXR), which suppresses the proinflammatory NF-κB pathway. Clinically, these effects make rifaximin effective in treating conditions like IBS-D and potentially other inflammatory bowel disorders.

Roxadustat.name: Roxadustat
Roxadustat.description: Roxadustat is a hypoxia-inducible factor prolyl hydroxylase inhibitor. It is indicated for the treatment of anemia in patients with chronic kidney disease. The drug increases red blood cell production by stabilizing HIF, a transcription factor activated under low oxygen conditions. Roxadustat received its first approval from the European Commission in August 2021.
Roxadustat.mechanism_of_action: Roxadustat is a reversible inhibitor of HIF-prolyl hydroxylase (HIF-PHD) enzymes. By inhibiting HIF-PHD, it stabilizes hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF), leading to increased endogenous erythropoietin (EPO) production and enhanced erythropoiesis. Roxadustat also suppresses hepcidin and regulates iron transporter proteins, improving iron absorption and mobilization. Overall, it increases hemoglobin production and red blood cell mass in patients with anemia due to chronic kidney disease.
Roxadustat.pharmacodynamics: Roxadustat increases iron bioavailability and stimulates hemoglobin production, leading to greater red blood cell mass in anemic patients. In non-dialysis-dependent CKD patients, it maintains hemoglobin levels for up to two years. Its efficacy in achieving hemoglobin response is comparable to erythropoietin-stimulating agents. Roxadustat also lowers cholesterol levels, independent of other lipid-lowering therapies.

Tranilast.name: Tranilast
Tranilast.description: Tranilast is an antiallergic drug developed by Kissei Pharmaceuticals. It is primarily indicated for bronchial asthma, keloids, and hypertrophic scars. Tranilast is also used to treat allergic disorders like allergic rhinitis and atopic dermatitis. Its key effects include reducing allergic inflammation and suppressing abnormal scar formation.

Olanzapine.name: Olanzapine
Olanzapine.description: Olanzapine is an atypical antipsychotic medication belonging to the thienobenzodiazepine class. It is mainly indicated for the treatment of schizophrenia and bipolar disorder. The drug is known for its strong efficacy and lower risk of extrapyramidal side effects compared to older antipsychotics. Olanzapine was approved for use in the US in 1996.
Olanzapine.mechanism_of_action: Olanzapine acts primarily as an antagonist at multiple neuronal receptors, including dopamine (D1-D4) and serotonin (5HT2A, 5HT2C, 5HT3, 5HT6) receptors. Its main therapeutic effect is due to blocking dopamine D2 receptors in the mesolimbic pathway, reducing dopamine activity at post-synaptic sites. Olanzapine also antagonizes serotonin 5HT2A receptors in the frontal cortex, which helps lower the risk of certain side effects. Additionally, it blocks alpha-1 adrenergic, histamine H1, and muscarinic receptors.
Olanzapine.pharmacodynamics: Olanzapine primarily acts by blocking dopamine D2 receptors, reducing positive symptoms of psychosis such as hallucinations and delusions, and by antagonizing serotonin 5HT2A receptors, which helps prevent negative symptoms like anhedonia and poor attention. Its higher affinity for D2 receptors compared to other dopamine subtypes is associated with fewer side effects. Clinically, olanzapine is effective in treating schizophrenia and bipolar disorder in adults and acute manic or mixed episodes in adolescents. Additionally, it significantly improves control of chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting, with high rates of complete response in clinical trials.

Decitabine.name: Decitabine
Decitabine.description: Decitabine is a nucleoside analogue used primarily for the treatment of myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS). It works by integrating into DNA and inhibiting DNA methyltransferases, resulting in global DNA hypomethylation and therapeutic effects. Decitabine helps manage symptoms like neutropenia and thrombocytopenia and may delay progression to acute myeloid leukemia. It is available as Dacogen® and also in an oral combination with cedazuridine.
Decitabine.mechanism_of_action: Decitabine is a prodrug that is phosphorylated inside cells and incorporated into DNA during replication. It traps DNA methyltransferase enzymes (DNMTs), leading to their depletion and causing global DNA hypomethylation. This hypomethylation reactivates silenced tumor suppressor genes and disrupts neoplastic cell proliferation. At higher concentrations, decitabine also induces DNA damage and cell death.
Decitabine.pharmacodynamics: Decitabine is a prodrug that, once phosphorylated, incorporates into DNA and alters gene expression. Its use commonly results in neutropenia and thrombocytopenia. Decitabine can cause fetal harm, so effective contraception is advised during treatment. Clinical monitoring for blood cell counts and pregnancy status is essential.

Entrectinib.name: Entrectinib
Entrectinib.description: Entrectinib is a kinase inhibitor targeting TRK (TRKA, TRKB, TRKC), ROS1, and ALK proteins. It is mainly indicated for ROS1-positive metastatic non-small cell lung cancer and NTRK gene fusion-positive solid tumors. The drug is approved as a last-line therapy based on accelerated approval from early clinical data. Entrectinib offers a broader range of targets compared to other ALK inhibitors like alectinib, ceritinib, and lorlatinib.
Entrectinib.mechanism_of_action: Entrectinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor that targets TRKA, TRKB, TRKC, ROS1, and ALK receptors. It acts as an ATP competitor, blocking kinase activity and downstream signaling pathways such as MAPK, PI3K, PLC-γ, and JAK/STAT. This inhibition suppresses cancer cell proliferation and promotes apoptosis. As a result, tumor volume is reduced.
Entrectinib.pharmacodynamics: Entrectinib and its active metabolite inhibit multiple pathways involved in cell survival and proliferation. This leads to increased apoptosis, reducing cancer cell growth. Clinically, these actions help shrink tumors. The overall effect supports its use in targeted cancer therapy.

Apalutamide.name: Apalutamide
Apalutamide.description: Apalutamide is an oral androgen receptor antagonist used primarily to treat non-metastatic, castration-resistant prostate cancer. It works by blocking androgen receptor signaling, thereby inhibiting tumor growth driven by androgens. Clinical studies show it significantly prolongs metastasis-free survival and reduces prostate-specific antigen (PSA) levels in affected patients. Approved by the FDA in 2018 as Erleada, apalutamide has demonstrated good tolerability and safety.
Apalutamide.mechanism_of_action: Apalutamide is an androgen receptor (AR) antagonist that binds directly to the AR ligand-binding domain. This binding disrupts AR signaling, inhibits DNA binding, and blocks AR-mediated gene transcription. Apalutamide also prevents AR translocation to the nucleus, reducing its interaction with androgen response elements. Its main metabolite, N-desmethyl apalutamide, is a less potent AR inhibitor.
Apalutamide.pharmacodynamics: Apalutamide shows 7 to 10 times greater affinity for androgen receptors than bicalutamide, and retains full antagonistic activity even in AR-overexpressing, bicalutamide-resistant cell lines. In mouse models, apalutamide induces significant tumor regression, reduces tumor cell proliferation by 60%, and increases apoptosis tenfold compared to control. Clinical studies indicate apalutamide has antitumor effects in prostate cancer but may cause a concentration-dependent increase in QTcF interval. Overall, apalutamide demonstrates superior efficacy over bicalutamide in preclinical models.

Voriconazole.name: Voriconazole
Voriconazole.description: Voriconazole (Vfend) is a triazole antifungal medication. It is primarily indicated for serious invasive fungal infections, such as invasive candidiasis and aspergillosis, especially in immunocompromised patients. The drug is effective against some fluconazole-resistant organisms due to its high affinity for 14-alpha sterol demethylase. Voriconazole was FDA-approved in 2002.
Voriconazole.mechanism_of_action: Voriconazole is a triazole antifungal that targets fungal pathogens like Aspergillus and Candida species. It inhibits 14-alpha sterol demethylase (CYP51), blocking the demethylation of lanosterol in the ergosterol synthesis pathway. This disruption reduces ergosterol levels, impairing fungal cell membrane function and limiting cell growth. As a result, the host immune system can more effectively eliminate the infection.
Voriconazole.pharmacodynamics: Voriconazole is a triazole antifungal that inhibits fungal growth, providing fungistatic effects against various infections. Clinically, it is effective in treating serious fungal diseases. However, it may cause hepatotoxicity and photosensitivity in some patients. Monitoring for liver function and skin reactions is recommended during therapy.

Drospirenone.name: Drospirenone
Drospirenone.description: Drospirenone is a synthetic progestin used primarily in oral contraceptives, often combined with estrogens like ethinyl estradiol or estetrol. It is indicated for contraception, acne control, and management of premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD). Key effects include prevention of pregnancy and improvement of acne and PMDD symptoms. There are safety concerns about an increased risk of venous thromboembolism, though evidence is conflicting and the risk remains lower than during pregnancy or postpartum.
Drospirenone.mechanism_of_action: Drospirenone and ethinyl estradiol suppress FSH and LH secretion, preventing ovulation as their primary contraceptive mechanism. They also thicken cervical mucus and alter the endometrium, reducing sperm movement and implantation likelihood. Drospirenone acts as an anti-mineralocorticoid, blocking aldosterone receptors to promote sodium and water excretion. Additionally, drospirenone has antiandrogenic effects, inhibiting androgen receptor binding and synthesis, which helps treat acne and hirsutism.
Drospirenone.pharmacodynamics: Drospirenone inhibits follicle maturation and ovulation, preventing pregnancy. It has antiandrogen effects that help improve acne and hirsutism, and when combined with ethinyl estradiol, it may benefit plasma lipid profiles. Drospirenone is associated with fewer progesterone-related side effects like breast tenderness and mood swings. However, it increases the risk of venous thromboembolism, especially in smokers and women over 35, and can cause hyperkalemia due to its antimineralcorticoid effects.

Dexamethasone.name: Dexamethasone
Dexamethasone.description: Dexamethasone is a synthetic corticosteroid used to treat a wide range of conditions, including endocrine, rheumatic, allergic, and respiratory disorders. It was approved by the FDA in 1958 and is structurally related to hydrocortisone and prednisolone. Key effects include potent anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions. Notably, dexamethasone has been shown to reduce mortality in severe COVID-19 patients requiring ventilation or oxygen.
Dexamethasone.mechanism_of_action: Corticosteroids bind to the glucocorticoid receptor, altering gene expression to reduce inflammation. They decrease capillary permeability, leukocyte migration, and inhibit key inflammatory pathways such as phospholipase A2 and NF-kappa B. At lower doses, they act as anti-inflammatory agents, while higher doses suppress the immune system. Prolonged high doses can also activate mineralocorticoid receptors, affecting sodium and potassium balance.
Dexamethasone.pharmacodynamics: Corticosteroids bind to glucocorticoid receptors, suppressing pro-inflammatory signals and enhancing anti-inflammatory effects. Dexamethasone’s duration of action depends on the administration route. These drugs have a wide therapeutic window, often requiring doses much higher than physiological levels. Patients should be warned about risks of HPA axis suppression and increased infection susceptibility.

venetoclax.name: Venetoclax
venetoclax.description: Venetoclax is a BCL-2 inhibitor used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) and small lymphocytic lymphoma (SLL). It works by promoting apoptosis in cancer cells, making it highly effective, especially in CLL. Venetoclax is about 10 times more potent than navitoclax, its predecessor. Its indications have expanded to include patients with or without 17p deletion who have received at least one prior therapy.
venetoclax.mechanism_of_action: Venetoclax is a selective inhibitor of the BCL-2 protein, a key regulator of apoptosis. By binding to BCL-2, venetoclax displaces pro-apoptotic proteins, triggering mitochondrial membrane permeabilization and caspase activation. This restores programmed cell death in cancer cells that overexpress BCL-2. Unlike earlier inhibitors, venetoclax spares BCL-xL, reducing the risk of thrombocytopenia.
venetoclax.pharmacodynamics: Venetoclax selectively targets BCL2, inducing rapid apoptosis of CLL cells, often within 24 hours, which can lead to tumor lysis syndrome. It has shown significant efficacy in relapsed or refractory CLL and SLL, including in patients with poor prognostic factors such as deletion 17p, with overall response rates around 80%. The safety profile is considered manageable, and venetoclax does not significantly affect the cardiac QT interval. Its clinical benefits extend to various lymphoid malignancies.

Mebendazole.name: Mebendazole
Mebendazole.description: A benzimidazole that acts by interfering with carbohydrate metabolism and inhibiting polymerization of microtubules. [PubChem]
Mebendazole.mechanism_of_action: Mebendazole binds to tubulin in parasitic worms, inhibiting microtubule formation. This disrupts glucose uptake and depletes glycogen stores in the parasite. Cellular degeneration reduces ATP production, leading to energy loss. As a result, the parasite becomes immobilized and dies.
Mebendazole.pharmacodynamics: Mebendazole is a synthetic, broad-spectrum anthelmintic. It works by inhibiting tubulin polymerization, leading to the loss of cytoplasmic microtubules in parasites. This disruption impairs glucose uptake and essential cellular functions, resulting in parasite death. Clinically, it is effective against a range of intestinal helminth infections.

Macitentan.name: Macitentan
Macitentan.description: Macitentan is a dual endothelin receptor antagonist used to treat pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH). It was first approved by the FDA in 2013 and has a lower risk of liver toxicity compared to bosentan. A combination product with tadalafil (Opsynvi) is also approved for PAH treatment in Canada and the US. Macitentan helps improve exercise capacity and delays disease progression in PAH patients.
Macitentan.mechanism_of_action: Macitentan is an antagonist of endothelin A (E<sub>A</sub>) and B (E<sub>B</sub>) receptors, blocking the effects of endothelin-1 and -2. By inhibiting E<sub>A</sub> receptor signaling on pulmonary artery smooth muscle cells, it reduces vasoconstriction and smooth muscle proliferation, key factors in pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH). Although it also blocks E<sub>B</sub> receptors, the main therapeutic effect comes from E<sub>A</sub> blockade, as E<sub>B</sub> signaling is associated with vasodilation. Overall, macitentan decreases pulmonary vascular resistance and slows disease progression in PAH.
Macitentan.pharmacodynamics: Macitentan acts primarily by reducing vasoconstriction and cell proliferation due to endothelin overexpression.[L35890, L39105]

Relugolix.name: Relugolix
Relugolix.description: Relugolix is an orally administered gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) receptor antagonist. It is mainly indicated for advanced prostate cancer, uterine fibroids, and has also been studied for endometriosis. Key effects include effective suppression of testosterone levels and management of hormone-responsive symptoms. Its oral formulation offers a convenient alternative to injectable therapies, reducing the need for clinic visits.
Relugolix.mechanism_of_action: Relugolix acts as a competitive antagonist at GnRH receptors in the pituitary gland. By blocking these receptors, it reduces the release of luteinizing hormone (LH). Lower LH levels lead to decreased testosterone production in the testes. This reduction in testosterone helps slow the progression of prostate cancer.
Relugolix.pharmacodynamics: Relugolix achieves castrate-level testosterone concentrations in 56% of patients by day 4, with 97% maintaining these levels through 48 weeks using once-daily oral dosing. Androgen deprivation therapies, including relugolix, may prolong the QTc interval and should be used cautiously in patients at risk for QTc prolongation. Relugolix may cause fetal harm if administered during pregnancy, so effective contraception is recommended for male patients with female partners during and for 2 weeks after therapy. These effects support its use in prostate cancer but require careful patient selection and counseling.

Oxaliplatin.name: Oxaliplatin
Oxaliplatin.description: Oxaliplatin is a platinum-based chemotherapy drug primarily used to treat colorectal cancer, often as part of the FOLFOX regimen with fluorouracil and leucovorin. Its main mechanism is DNA crosslinking, leading to tumor cell death, and it has greater cytotoxic effects compared to other platinum drugs due to its unique chemical structure. Oxaliplatin is also being studied for use in other cancers, such as non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma, breast cancer, mesothelioma, and non-small cell lung cancer. It was FDA-approved in 2004 and is marketed as Eloxatin®.
Oxaliplatin.mechanism_of_action: Oxaliplatin is converted in the body to active platinum derivatives by displacement of its oxalate ligand. These derivatives form covalent crosslinks with DNA, primarily at the N7 positions of guanine and adenine bases. The resulting inter- and intrastrand DNA crosslinks inhibit DNA replication and transcription. This cytotoxic effect is cell-cycle nonspecific.
Oxaliplatin.pharmacodynamics: Oxaliplatin demonstrates antitumor activity against colon carcinoma in vivo. When combined with fluorouracil, it shows enhanced antiproliferative effects compared to either drug alone. This synergistic activity is observed in multiple tumor models, including colon, mammary, and leukemia. The combination leads to improved clinical outcomes in cancer treatment.

Futibatinib.name: Futibatinib
Futibatinib.description: Futibatinib is a novel inhibitor of Fibroblast Growth Factor Receptor (FGFR), a key regulator in cell growth and survival. It is primarily indicated for the treatment of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, a type of liver cancer, especially in cases with FGFR genomic aberrations. The drug works by blocking FGFR signalling pathways, which are often dysregulated in certain cancers. Futibatinib received FDA approval in September 2022 and conditional marketing authorization in Europe in July 2023 for this indication.
Futibatinib.mechanism_of_action: Futibatinib is a selective, irreversible inhibitor of FGFR1-4. It forms a covalent bond with cysteine in the ATP-binding pocket of FGFR, blocking receptor phosphorylation. This inhibition disrupts downstream signaling pathways such as MAPK, PI3K/Akt/mTOR, PLCγ, and JAK/STAT. As a result, futibatinib reduces the viability of cancer cells with FGFR alterations.
Futibatinib.pharmacodynamics: Futibatinib is an anticancer agent that shows significant anti-tumor activity in animal models with FGFR genetic alterations. It works by suppressing tumor growth in a dose-dependent manner. Futibatinib does not affect cell lines lacking FGFR genomic aberrations. Its clinical benefit is primarily seen in cancers with activating FGFR mutations.

Tofacitinib.name: Tofacitinib
Tofacitinib.description: Tofacitinib is a Janus kinase inhibitor that modulates immune cell function. It is primarily approved for treating moderate to severe rheumatoid arthritis in patients who do not respond well to or cannot tolerate methotrexate. The drug is also being studied for organ transplant rejection and psoriasis. Key side effects include nausea, headache, and potentially serious immune and blood-related adverse effects.
Tofacitinib.mechanism_of_action: Tofacitinib is a partial and reversible inhibitor of Janus kinases (JAKs), key enzymes in the JAK-STAT signaling pathway. By blocking JAKs, tofacitinib prevents phosphorylation and activation of STATs, reducing transcription of genes involved in immune cell function and inflammation. This inhibition decreases the body's response to pro-inflammatory cytokines, thereby reducing tissue inflammation and joint damage in rheumatoid arthritis. Tofacitinib may also exert effects through additional, non-JAK-STAT pathways.
Tofacitinib.pharmacodynamics: Tofacitinib inhibits janus kinases, reducing inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis and leading to improved ACR20 responses and physical functioning compared to placebo. Common adverse effects include headache, diarrhea, nausea, and upper respiratory infections, while serious risks involve immunologic and hematologic issues such as lymphopenia, neutropenia, anemia, and increased cancer and infection risk. Patients must be screened for latent infections before starting therapy and closely monitored for infection and blood abnormalities during treatment. Tofacitinib rapidly lowers CRP levels and alters immune cell counts, with pharmacodynamic effects persisting beyond its pharmacokinetic half-life.

Fluvoxamine.name: Fluvoxamine
Fluvoxamine.description: Fluvoxamine is an antidepressant classified as a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI). It is primarily indicated for the treatment of obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD). The drug has been widely used since the 1980s, with extensive clinical experience and millions of patients treated worldwide. Its key effect is to increase serotonin levels in the brain, helping to alleviate symptoms of OCD.
Fluvoxamine.mechanism_of_action: Fluvoxamine primarily works by inhibiting the reuptake of serotonin in the central nervous system, increasing serotonin levels at synaptic sites. This enhances serotonin activity, particularly at 5HT<sub>1A</sub> autoreceptors. Fluvoxamine shows minimal affinity for adrenergic, muscarinic, dopaminergic, histaminergic, GABAergic, opiate, and most serotonin receptors, but does bind to σ1 receptors. Its main target is the serotonin reuptake pump on neuronal membranes.
Fluvoxamine.pharmacodynamics: Fluvoxamine is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor (SSRI) used primarily to treat depression, anxiety, and body dysmorphic disorder. Its clinical effects are mainly due to potent inhibition of neuronal serotonin reuptake, with minimal impact on norepinephrine and dopamine reuptake. Fluvoxamine shows little affinity for other neurotransmitter receptors, reducing the risk of anticholinergic, sedative, and cardiovascular side effects. Chronic use may affect norepinephrine receptor regulation, though findings are mixed.

Selinexor.name: Selinexor
Selinexor.description: Selinexor is a first-in-class selective inhibitor of nuclear transport (SINE) used as an anti-cancer agent. It is approved for treating multiple myeloma in combination with bortezomib and dexamethasone, especially in patients who have received prior therapies. Selinexor also has accelerated approval for adults with relapsed or refractory diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) after at least two prior treatments. Its key effects include inhibiting cancer cell growth by blocking nuclear export mechanisms.
Selinexor.mechanism_of_action: Selinexor inhibits exportin-1 (XPO1), a nuclear export protein responsible for transporting tumor suppressor proteins and other regulatory factors out of the nucleus. By blocking XPO1, selinexor causes these proteins, such as p53 and p21, to accumulate in the nucleus, enhancing their tumor-suppressing activity. This inhibition also reduces the export of oncogenic mRNAs and antiapoptotic proteins, leading to cell cycle arrest and increased apoptosis in cancer cells. The overall effect is the suppression of cancer cell growth and survival.
Selinexor.pharmacodynamics: Selinexor causes cell cycle arrest and apoptosis in cancer cells.[L10145]

Sildenafil.name: Sildenafil
Sildenafil.description: Sildenafil is a phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5) inhibitor used primarily to treat erectile dysfunction and pulmonary arterial hypertension. It works by preventing the breakdown of cGMP, leading to smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation in the penis and pulmonary vasculature. This results in improved erectile function and reduced pulmonary blood pressure. Sildenafil is widely available as both a generic and under brand names like Viagra, but can cause dangerous drops in blood pressure when combined with certain heart medications.
Sildenafil.mechanism_of_action: Sildenafil is a selective inhibitor of phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE5), primarily found in the corpus cavernosum and pulmonary vasculature. By inhibiting PDE5, sildenafil prevents the breakdown of cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP), enhancing the effects of nitric oxide (NO) released during sexual stimulation. This leads to increased cGMP levels, resulting in smooth muscle relaxation and increased blood flow to the penis, restoring erectile function. In the pulmonary vasculature, sildenafil also causes vasodilation, benefiting patients with pulmonary arterial hypertension.
Sildenafil.pharmacodynamics: Sildenafil is a selective inhibitor of phosphodiesterase-5 (PDE5), with much greater potency for PDE5 over other phosphodiesterases, including a 10-fold selectivity over PDE6 in the retina. Clinically, sildenafil improves erectile function in men with erectile dysfunction, with effects generally observed within 60 minutes and lasting up to 4 hours. It causes mild, transient decreases in blood pressure without significant clinical consequences, and does not produce clinically relevant ECG changes at recommended doses. Mild, transient disturbances in color discrimination (blue/green) may occur, but sildenafil does not affect visual acuity or contrast sensitivity.

Ivermectin.name: Ivermectin
Ivermectin.description: Ivermectin is a semi-synthetic antiparasitic drug derived from avermectins produced by Streptomyces avermitilis. It is primarily indicated for treating onchocerciasis and other worm infestations, and can also be used topically for head lice. Ivermectin gained attention for off-label use in COVID-19, but current evidence for its effectiveness and safety in this indication is insufficient and unreliable. Until robust clinical data are available, ivermectin should not be used for COVID-19 treatment or prevention.
Ivermectin.mechanism_of_action: Ivermectin binds with high affinity to glutamate-gated chloride channels in invertebrate nerve and muscle cells, increasing chloride ion influx. This causes cell hyperpolarization, leading to paralysis and death of the parasite. It may also act as a GABA agonist, disrupting parasite nervous system function. Additionally, ivermectin may impair microfilarial development and inhibit their release from female worms.
Ivermectin.pharmacodynamics: Ivermectin is a semisynthetic anthelmintic agent from the avermectin class, derived from _Streptomyces avermitilis_. It acts as a broad-spectrum anti-parasitic, effective against various helminths and ectoparasites. Its pharmacodynamic effects result in paralysis and death of susceptible parasites. Clinically, ivermectin is used to treat infections such as onchocerciasis, strongyloidiasis, and other parasitic diseases.

Ixazomib.name: Ixazomib
Ixazomib.description: Ixazomib is an oral, reversible proteasome inhibitor, primarily composed of the R-enantiomer of a modified peptide boronic acid. It is mainly indicated for the treatment of multiple myeloma. The drug works by inhibiting proteasome activity, leading to cancer cell death. Ixazomib was the first oral proteasome inhibitor approved in the US and has also been investigated for other hematological and immune-related conditions.
Ixazomib.mechanism_of_action: Ixazomib is a selective and reversible inhibitor of the 20S proteasome, a key component of the ubiquitin-proteasome pathway responsible for protein degradation. It primarily targets the β5 chymotrypsin-like proteolytic site, blocking its activity at low concentrations. At higher concentrations, ixazomib can also inhibit the β1 (caspase-like) and β2 (trypsin-like) sites. By inhibiting proteasome function, ixazomib disrupts cellular homeostasis, leading to apoptosis in malignant cells.
Ixazomib.pharmacodynamics: Ixazomib is a reversible proteasome inhibitor that induces apoptosis and cytotoxicity in multiple myeloma cells, including those resistant to prior therapies. It shows synergistic effects when combined with lenalidomide. In vivo studies demonstrate antitumor activity in mouse myeloma models. Additionally, ixazomib exhibits antiangiogenic effects and inhibits osteoclast formation and bone resorption.

Vilanterol.name: Vilanterol
Vilanterol.description: Vilanterol is a long-acting β2-adrenergic agonist (LABA) used for once-daily treatment of COPD and asthma. It is highly selective for β2 receptors, providing rapid onset and prolonged bronchodilation. Vilanterol works by relaxing bronchial smooth muscle and reducing hypersensitivity mediator release in the lungs. It is available in combination products such as BREO ELLIPTA, ANORO ELLIPTA, and TRELEGY ELLIPTA, approved for maintenance therapy in COPD and, for some products, asthma.
Vilanterol.mechanism_of_action: Vilanterol is a long-acting beta2-adrenergic agonist that selectively stimulates beta2 receptors in the lungs. This activation increases intracellular adenylyl cyclase activity, raising cAMP levels. Elevated cAMP leads to relaxation of bronchial smooth muscle and reduces the release of hypersensitivity mediators from mast cells. The overall effect is improved airway function and reduced bronchoconstriction.

Thymopentin.name: Thymopentin
Thymopentin.description: Thymopentin has been used in trials studying the treatment and prevention of HIV Infections and Hepatocellular Carcinoma.

Orlistat.name: Orlistat
Orlistat.description: Orlistat is a lipase inhibitor used to treat obesity. It is indicated for weight loss in combination with a reduced-calorie diet, especially when diet and exercise alone are insufficient. Orlistat works by blocking fat absorption in the gut, leading to decreased calorie intake. It is available in both prescription and over-the-counter forms and is generally well tolerated.
Orlistat.mechanism_of_action: Orlistat inhibits gastric and pancreatic lipase enzymes in the gastrointestinal tract. It binds covalently to serine residues at the enzymes' active sites. This blocks the breakdown of dietary triglycerides into absorbable forms. As a result, fat absorption is reduced, aiding in weight loss and maintenance.
Orlistat.pharmacodynamics: Orlistat helps with weight reduction and maintenance by inhibiting the absorption of dietary fats via the inhibition of lipase enzymes.[A1734,A1735]

Hesperidin.name: Hesperidin
Hesperidin.description: Hesperidin is a flavan-on glycoside found in citrus fruits.

Ranolazine.name: Ranolazine
Ranolazine.description: Ranolazine is a piperazine derivative used to treat chronic angina. It is indicated for patients experiencing angina symptoms that interfere with daily activities. Ranolazine works through a unique mechanism different from other anti-anginal drugs. It is well-tolerated and was FDA-approved in 2006.
Ranolazine.mechanism_of_action: Ranolazine primarily inhibits the cardiac late sodium current (INa), helping to restore electrolyte balance in myocardial cells and relieve angina symptoms. It also inhibits sodium and potassium ion channel currents and has weak activity on L-type calcium channels, resulting in minimal direct vasodilation and little effect on AV nodal conduction. Additionally, ranolazine antagonizes alpha 1 and beta 1 adrenergic receptors and inhibits fatty acid oxidation. The exact clinical significance of these mechanisms in angina treatment is still being studied.
Ranolazine.pharmacodynamics: Ranolazine provides antianginal and anti-ischemic effects without lowering heart rate or blood pressure. It blocks the rapid delayed rectifier potassium current (IKr), leading to dose-dependent QTc interval prolongation. Ranolazine does not affect cardiac contractility, conduction, or rate at rest or during exercise. These properties make it beneficial for angina management without significant hemodynamic changes.

Lopinavir.name: Lopinavir
Lopinavir.description: Lopinavir is an antiretroviral protease inhibitor used to treat HIV-1 infection. It is always administered with ritonavir, which boosts lopinavir's effectiveness by inhibiting its metabolism. The combination prevents HIV-1 protease activity, blocking viral replication. Lopinavir/ritonavir was also investigated for COVID-19 treatment, but is primarily indicated for HIV-1.
Lopinavir.mechanism_of_action: Lopinavir inhibits the HIV-1 protease enzyme, which is essential for viral maturation. It mimics the natural substrate of the protease but cannot be cleaved, blocking the enzyme's activity. This inhibition prevents the cleavage of the Gag polyprotein, leading to the formation of immature, non-infectious viral particles. The main target of lopinavir is the HIV-1 protease.
Lopinavir.pharmacodynamics: Lopinavir inhibits a key enzyme essential for HIV replication, leading to antiviral effects. Its moderate duration of action requires once or twice daily dosing. Lopinavir commonly interacts with other drugs, so caution is needed when used with additional medications. Serious adverse effects, including fatal hepatotoxicity and pancreatitis, may occur, especially in high-risk patients who require close monitoring.

Filgotinib.name: Filgotinib
Filgotinib.description: Filgotinib is a highly selective Janus kinase 1 (JAK1) inhibitor used to treat rheumatoid arthritis (RA). It is indicated for patients who cannot tolerate or have not responded to disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drugs (DMARDs). Filgotinib targets inflammatory pathways to reduce joint symptoms and improve clinical outcomes. Its selectivity for JAK1 is designed to enhance safety while maintaining efficacy compared to less selective JAK inhibitors.
Filgotinib.mechanism_of_action: Filgotinib selectively inhibits the Janus kinase 1 (JAK1) enzyme, showing about 30-fold higher selectivity for JAK1 over JAK2. By blocking JAK1 phosphorylation, it prevents activation of the STAT pathway. This inhibition reduces proinflammatory cytokine signaling involved in inflammatory diseases like rheumatoid arthritis. Ultimately, filgotinib helps decrease disease progression by lowering matrix metalloproteinase levels and promoting cell apoptosis in affected tissues.
