Filgotinib.pharmacodynamics: Filgotinib inhibits Janus kinase 1 (JAK1), reducing pro-inflammatory cytokine signaling. It specifically blocks IL-6-induced STAT1 phosphorylation. This results in lowered serum C-reactive protein levels. Clinically, these effects contribute to decreased inflammation.

Hydroxyurea.name: Hydroxyurea
Hydroxyurea.description: Hydroxyurea is a non-alkylating antineoplastic agent. It is mainly indicated for the treatment of sickle cell anemia in adults. The drug can be effective but is used cautiously due to the need for close monitoring and risks of serious side effects, including secondary cancers and birth defects. Limited clinical use is also due to lack of awareness and adherence.
Hydroxyurea.mechanism_of_action: Hydroxyurea inhibits DNA synthesis by blocking ribonucleotide reductase, particularly affecting rapidly proliferating cells. It reduces the tyrosyl free radical at the enzyme’s active site, preventing conversion of ribonucleotides to deoxyribonucleotides. In cancer therapy, it sensitizes cells to radiation by arresting them in the G1 phase and hindering DNA repair. In sickle cell disease, hydroxyurea increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF) levels, reduces sickling, and may act via nitric oxide-mediated pathways.
Hydroxyurea.pharmacodynamics: The correlation between hydroxyurea concentrations, reduction of crisis rate, and increase in HbF, is not known.[L49181]

Verteporfin.name: Verteporfin
Verteporfin.description: Verteporfin (Visudyne) is a benzoporphyrin derivative used as a photosensitizer in photodynamic therapy. Its main indication is the treatment of wet age-related macular degeneration by targeting abnormal blood vessels in the eye. When activated by nonthermal red light, verteporfin generates reactive oxygen species that damage and block these vessels. This helps prevent further vision loss associated with the disease.
Verteporfin.mechanism_of_action: Verteporfin is transported in plasma mainly by lipoproteins and preferentially accumulates in neovascular tissues. Upon activation by light in the presence of oxygen, it generates singlet oxygen and reactive oxygen radicals. These reactive species cause local damage to neovascular endothelium, leading to vessel occlusion through platelet aggregation and fibrin clot formation. The cytotoxic effects of verteporfin can also be used to destroy tumor cells.
Verteporfin.pharmacodynamics: Verteporfin is a photosensitizing agent used with laser therapy to target abnormal eye blood vessels in conditions like wet macular degeneration. It selectively accumulates in these vessels and, upon activation by red light, generates reactive oxygen species. This leads to localized endothelial damage and vessel occlusion. The clinical outcome is the reduction or elimination of abnormal blood vessel growth, helping to preserve vision.

Clotrimazole.name: Clotrimazole
Clotrimazole.description: Clotrimazole is a broad-spectrum antifungal agent from the imidazole class of azoles. It is mainly indicated for treating fungal infections such as vaginal yeast infections and athlete’s foot. The drug is available in creams, pessaries, and troche formulations. Clotrimazole is well tolerated with minimal side effects and is also being studied for potential use in other diseases like sickle cell disease, malaria, and some cancers.
Clotrimazole.mechanism_of_action: Clotrimazole inhibits ergosterol biosynthesis in fungal cell membranes by blocking lanosterol 14-demethylase (CYP51). This disruption compromises membrane integrity, leading to impaired fungal growth. Additionally, clotrimazole inhibits sarcoplasmic reticulum Ca2+-ATPase and various calcium channels, affecting cellular calcium regulation. These secondary actions contribute to non-antifungal pharmacological effects.
Clotrimazole.pharmacodynamics: Clotrimazole is a broad-spectrum antifungal that works by altering fungal cell membrane permeability, inhibiting yeast growth. It is primarily fungistatic at standard concentrations but can be fungicidal against Candida species at higher levels. Clinically, it is effective against various pathogenic yeasts, though resistance has become increasingly common. Overall, clotrimazole’s efficacy may be reduced in populations with resistant fungal strains.

Capivasertib.name: Capivasertib
Capivasertib.description: Capivasertib (TRUQAP) is a pan-AKT kinase inhibitor approved for HR-positive, HER2-negative locally advanced or metastatic breast cancer with PIK3CA, AKT1, or PTEN gene alterations. It is used in combination with fulvestrant for adult patients whose cancer has become resistant to endocrine therapy. Capivasertib targets the PIK3/AKT pathway, a key driver in breast cancer progression. In clinical trials, this combination reduced the risk of disease progression or death by 50% compared to fulvestrant alone.
Capivasertib.mechanism_of_action: Capivasertib inhibits all three isoforms of the serine/threonine kinase AKT (AKT1, AKT2, AKT3). By blocking AKT activity, it prevents phosphorylation of downstream AKT substrates. This disrupts signaling pathways often activated in tumors due to mutations in AKT1, loss of PTEN, or mutations in PIK3CA. The result is reduced tumor cell survival and proliferation.
Capivasertib.pharmacodynamics: Capivasertib inhibits growth of breast cancer cell lines with PIK3CA, AKT1 mutations, or PTEN alterations, both in vitro and in vivo. It shows enhanced tumor suppression when combined with fulvestrant, especially in estrogen receptor-positive models. Exposure-response relationships exist for adverse effects such as diarrhea, rash, and hyperglycemia at various doses. No clinically significant QTc interval prolongation was observed at the recommended dose.

Tadalafil.name: Tadalafil
Tadalafil.description: Tadalafil is a selective phosphodiesterase-5 (PDE5) inhibitor. It is mainly indicated for erectile dysfunction, pulmonary arterial hypertension, and benign prostatic hypertrophy. Compared to other PDE5 inhibitors, tadalafil has greater selectivity and a longer half-life, allowing for convenient once-daily dosing in PAH. Its key effects include improved blood flow and relaxation of smooth muscle.
Tadalafil.mechanism_of_action: Tadalafil is a selective inhibitor of phosphodiesterase-5 (PDE5), which increases cyclic guanosine monophosphate (cGMP) levels, leading to smooth muscle relaxation. In erectile dysfunction (ED), this promotes penile smooth muscle relaxation and enhances blood flow, facilitating erection during sexual arousal. For pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), tadalafil boosts NO-cGMP signaling, resulting in vasodilation and reduced pulmonary blood pressure. In benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), its benefits are likely due to decreased inflammation and cell proliferation via the cGMP pathway, rather than direct smooth muscle relaxation.
Tadalafil.pharmacodynamics: Tadalafil increases smooth muscle relaxation in the penis during sexual stimulation, improving erectile function in ED. It also induces vasodilation in pulmonary arteries, lowering blood pressure in PAH. In BPH, tadalafil may reduce prostate size and relieve urinary symptoms by decreasing smooth muscle cell proliferation. Its lower affinity for PDE6 results in fewer visual side effects compared to other PDE5 inhibitors.

Aprepitant.name: Aprepitant
Aprepitant.description: Aprepitant is an antiemetic drug that acts as a selective antagonist of substance P/neurokinin 1 (NK1) receptors. It is primarily indicated for the prevention of acute and delayed nausea and vomiting associated with highly emetogenic cancer chemotherapy. Aprepitant is used in combination with other antiemetic agents. It has minimal affinity for serotonin, dopamine, and corticosteroid receptors, distinguishing it from other antiemetic therapies.
Aprepitant.mechanism_of_action: Aprepitant acts by blocking neurokinin-1 (NK1) receptors in the brain, which are involved in triggering nausea and vomiting. It crosses the blood-brain barrier and occupies these central NK1 receptors. By doing so, it inhibits both the acute and delayed phases of chemotherapy-induced emesis. Aprepitant also enhances the antiemetic effects of ondansetron (a 5-HT3 antagonist) and corticosteroids.
Aprepitant.pharmacodynamics: Aprepitant is a selective, high-affinity antagonist of substance P/neurokinin 1 (NK1) receptors. It is used with other antiemetics to prevent both acute and delayed nausea and vomiting from highly emetogenic chemotherapy. Unlike other therapies, aprepitant does not significantly affect serotonin, dopamine, or corticosteroid receptors. Its unique mechanism provides added efficacy in controlling chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting.

Arbutin.name: Arbutin
Arbutin.description: Arbutin is a compound extracted from the bearberry plant and other members of the Ericaceae family. It is mainly used as a skin-lightening agent in skincare products to treat hyperpigmentation by inhibiting melanin formation. Arbutin is also used as a urinary anti-infective and diuretic. It acts as a competitive inhibitor of tyrosinase and is less cytotoxic to melanocytes compared to similar agents.
Arbutin.mechanism_of_action: Arbutin acts primarily as a competitive inhibitor of the tyrosinase enzyme, targeting the L-tyrosine binding site. By inhibiting tyrosinase activity, it suppresses melanogenesis and reduces melanin production in skin cells. Most evidence indicates arbutin does not affect tyrosinase mRNA expression, though some studies suggest possible downregulation in certain cell systems. Its main depigmenting effect is through direct enzyme inhibition rather than gene expression changes.
Arbutin.pharmacodynamics: Arbutin inhibits tyrosinase activity and melanin synthesis in human keratinocytes and melanoma cells without significantly affecting cell growth or tyrosinase mRNA expression. It effectively reduces melanin production in a concentration-dependent manner and blocks α-MSH-stimulated melanogenesis in various models, including human skin tissue. Topical arbutin also inhibits UV-induced NF-κB activation in human keratinocytes and counteracts oxidative stress in mouse skin. These effects suggest arbutin’s potential for skin depigmentation and protection against oxidative damage.

Bendamustine.name: Bendamustine
Bendamustine.description: Bendamustine is a nitrogen mustard alkylating agent used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) and indolent B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL). It works by forming DNA crosslinks, leading to cell death. Bendamustine is effective against both dividing and non-dividing cells. The precise mechanism of action remains unclear.
Bendamustine.mechanism_of_action: Bendamustine is a bifunctional alkylating agent that forms electrophilic alkyl groups. These groups covalently bond to DNA, causing intra- and inter-strand crosslinks. This DNA damage leads to cell death in both dividing and non-dividing cells. The precise mechanism of action remains incompletely understood.
Bendamustine.pharmacodynamics: No mean changes in QTc interval greater than 20 milliseconds were detected up to one hour post-infusion.

Budesonide.name: Budesonide
Budesonide.description: Budesonide is a glucocorticoid medication used to treat inflammatory conditions like asthma, COPD, Crohn's disease, and ulcerative colitis. It works by reducing inflammation in the lungs and intestines. The drug was approved by the FDA in 1994 and is also available in combination with formoterol. Key effects include decreased symptoms of inflammation and improved respiratory function.
Budesonide.mechanism_of_action: Corticosteroids bind to glucocorticoid receptors, altering gene expression to reduce inflammation. They decrease capillary permeability, leukocyte migration, and inhibit key inflammatory mediators like phospholipase A2 and NF-kappa B. At lower doses, they act as anti-inflammatories, while higher doses suppress the immune system. Prolonged high doses can also activate mineralocorticoid receptors, affecting electrolyte balance by increasing sodium and decreasing potassium.
Budesonide.pharmacodynamics: Budesonide is a glucocorticoid that reduces inflammation in respiratory and digestive conditions. It has a wide therapeutic index, allowing for flexible dosing based on individual patient needs. Key risks include hypercorticism and adrenal axis suppression, so patient counseling is important. Clinical outcomes focus on effective symptom control with careful monitoring for adverse effects.

Glycyrrhizic acid.name: Glycyrrhizic acid
Glycyrrhizic acid.description: Glycyrrhizic acid is a compound extracted from the licorice root (_Glycyrrhiza glabra_) and is available as ammonium glycyrrhizin forms. It is primarily used as a hepatoprotective drug for chronic hepatitis in Japan and China. The compound also serves as a food sweetener, approved by the FDA. In Canada, it was previously approved for over-the-counter products, but these approvals have since been canceled.
Glycyrrhizic acid.mechanism_of_action: Glycyrrhizic acid exerts anti-inflammatory effects mainly by suppressing TNF-alpha, caspase 3, and inhibiting NFkB nuclear translocation. It also inhibits CD4+ T cell proliferation through JNK, ERK, and PI3K/AKT pathways. Its antiviral activity is broad-spectrum, blocking replication of various viruses and modulating immune responses. Additionally, glycyrrhizic acid inhibits 11-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 1, affecting steroid metabolism, and may induce lipoprotein lipase in non-hepatic tissues.
Glycyrrhizic acid.pharmacodynamics: Glycyrrhizic acid exhibits antiallergic, antiviral, and anti-inflammatory effects. It also improves glucose tolerance in clinical settings. In metabolic syndrome, glycyrrhizic acid significantly lowers blood glucose, fasting blood glucose, and mean serum insulin levels. These actions contribute to its potential therapeutic benefits in metabolic and inflammatory conditions.

Ligustrazine.name: Ligustrazine
Ligustrazine.description: Ligustrazine is under investigation in clinical trial NCT05942352 (Clinical Study of Ligustrazine in Treating Alcohol Addiction).

Pemetrexed.name: Pemetrexed
Pemetrexed.description: Pemetrexed (brand name Alimta) is a chemotherapy drug produced by Eli Lilly. It is primarily indicated for use with cisplatin to treat malignant pleural mesothelioma in patients not eligible for surgery. The drug has also been studied for non-small cell lung cancer. Pemetrexed was FDA-approved in February 2004.
Pemetrexed.mechanism_of_action: Pemetrexed is an antifolate chemotherapy drug that disrupts folate-dependent metabolic processes necessary for cell replication. It primarily inhibits the enzymes thymidylate synthase (TS), dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR), and glycinamide ribonucleotide formyltransferase (GARFT), which are crucial for DNA and purine synthesis. After entering cells via folate transporters, pemetrexed is converted to polyglutamate forms, which are retained longer in cells and enhance inhibition of TS and GARFT. This results in prolonged suppression of nucleotide synthesis, particularly in tumor cells.
Pemetrexed.pharmacodynamics: Pemetrexed inhibits the growth of mesothelioma cell lines and demonstrates synergistic effects when used with cisplatin. The depth of neutrophil count reduction (ANC nadir) is linked to pemetrexed exposure and is influenced by folic acid and vitamin B12 supplementation. No cumulative decrease in ANC nadir is observed with repeated pemetrexed cycles. These findings support its use in combination regimens and highlight the importance of supportive supplementation.

Pemigatinib.name: Pemigatinib
Pemigatinib.description: Pemigatinib (Pemazyre) is a small molecule kinase inhibitor that targets fibroblast growth factor receptors (FGFRs). It is primarily indicated for adults with unresectable locally advanced or metastatic cholangiocarcinoma harboring FGFR2 gene fusions or rearrangements, as detected by an FDA-approved test. The drug works by blocking FGFR signaling, which can reduce tumor growth and progression. Pemigatinib is available as oral tablets and was granted accelerated FDA approval based on clinical response rates.
Pemigatinib.mechanism_of_action: FGFRs are receptor tyrosine kinases that, upon binding fibroblast growth factors, dimerize and autophosphorylate, activating downstream signaling pathways like PI3K-AKT and RAS-MAPK. These pathways promote cell proliferation, survival, and differentiation. In certain tumors, genetic alterations in FGFR1, FGFR2, or FGFR3 cause constitutive activation, driving malignant cell growth. Pemigatinib inhibits FGFR1-3, blocking these pathways and reducing cancer cell viability in tumors with FGFR alterations.
Pemigatinib.pharmacodynamics: Pemigatinib is a potent small molecule inhibitor of FGFR1, FGFR2, and FGFR3, with an IC50 of less than 2 nM. It demonstrates strong anti-tumor activity in mouse xenograft models with FGFR alterations, including cholangiocarcinoma with FGFR2 fusion proteins. Pemigatinib also inhibits FGFR4, but at much higher concentrations. These effects support its clinical use in cancers driven by FGFR pathway alterations.

Plicamycin.name: Plicamycin
Plicamycin.description: Plicamycin is an antineoplastic antibiotic derived from Streptomyces plicatus. It was primarily used to treat testicular cancer and Paget's disease of bone, with occasional use for hypercalcemia management. The drug works by inhibiting cancer cell growth. Plicamycin was discontinued by its manufacturer in 2000.
Plicamycin.mechanism_of_action: Plicamycin inhibits cellular and enzymatic RNA synthesis by forming a complex with DNA. This action disrupts transcription processes in target cells. Additionally, plicamycin lowers serum calcium levels by inhibiting parathyroid hormone effects on osteoclasts and blocking vitamin D-induced hypercalcemia. Its main targets are DNA and pathways regulating calcium metabolism.
Plicamycin.pharmacodynamics: Plicamycin is highly cytotoxic to Hela cells, causing cell death within 48 hours at concentrations as low as 0.5 micrograms per milliliter. It demonstrates significant anti-tumor activity in experimental mouse models of leukemia when given intraperitoneally. These findings indicate strong in vitro and in vivo anti-cancer effects.

Sunitinib.name: Sunitinib
Sunitinib.description: Sunitinib is an orally administered, multi-targeted receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor. It is primarily indicated for the treatment of renal cell carcinoma (RCC) and imatinib-resistant gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST). The drug works by inhibiting several RTKs, including PDGF-R, VEGF-R, and KIT, which are involved in tumor growth and angiogenesis. Key effects include blocking cellular signaling pathways that promote cancer cell proliferation and survival.
Sunitinib.mechanism_of_action: Sunitinib is a small molecule inhibitor targeting multiple receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs) involved in cancer growth and angiogenesis. Its main targets include PDGFRα/β, VEGFR1-3, KIT, FLT3, CSF-1R, and RET. By inhibiting these RTKs, sunitinib blocks tumor cell proliferation and blood vessel formation. Its primary metabolite has similar inhibitory potency.
Sunitinib.pharmacodynamics: Sunitinib is an oral, small-molecule, multi-targeted receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK) inhibitor that was approved by the FDA on January 26, 2006.

Tazarotene.name: Tazarotene
Tazarotene.description: Tazarotene is an acetylenic retinoid available in topical formulations. It is primarily indicated for the treatment of psoriasis, acne, and photodamaged skin. As a prodrug, it is converted in the skin to its active form. Key effects include reducing inflammation and promoting skin cell turnover.
Tazarotene.mechanism_of_action: Tazarotene is converted to its active form, tazarotenic acid, which binds to retinoic acid receptors (RARs), with relative selectivity for RARβ and RARγ. This binding can modify gene expression involved in cell proliferation and differentiation. Tazarotenic acid also has some affinity for retinoid X receptors (RXRs). The exact mechanism is not fully understood, but these actions contribute to its therapeutic effects.
Tazarotene.pharmacodynamics: Tazarotene is converted to its active form, tazarotenic acid, after topical application. Clinically, it improves acne and signs of photoaging, such as mottling, hyperpigmentation, sallowness, and wrinkles. Long-term use reduces atypical melanocytes and keratinocytes, which are precursors to skin cancer. Additionally, it may enhance collagen production and organization in the skin.

Thymol.name: Thymol
Thymol.description: This drug is a phenol derived from thyme oil or other volatile oils. It is primarily used as a stabilizer in pharmaceutical preparations. Its main indications include antiseptic, antibacterial, and antifungal uses. It was also previously used to expel parasitic worms (vermifuge).

Belinostat.name: Belinostat
Belinostat.description: Belinostat is a histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor used as an anticancer agent. It is primarily indicated for the treatment of relapsed or refractory peripheral T-cell lymphoma (PTCL). The drug is administered intravenously as Beleodaq, typically in a 21-day cycle. Key effects include targeting hematological malignancies and solid tumors, and it is being studied in combination with other therapies.
Belinostat.mechanism_of_action: Belinostat inhibits histone deacetylase (HDAC), blocking the removal of acetyl groups from histones and other proteins. This leads to increased acetylation and upregulation of tumor-suppressor gene expression. The result is cell cycle arrest, reduced angiogenesis, and apoptosis in cancer cells. Its core target is HDAC enzymes.
Belinostat.pharmacodynamics: Beleodaq is a pan-HDAC inhibitor that induces tumor cell growth inhibition and apoptosis at low concentrations, particularly in PTCL cells. It demonstrates potent anti-cancer activity through epigenetic modulation. Clinical trials show no significant effects on heart rate, PR interval, or QRS duration. No cases of Torsades de Pointes were reported, indicating a favorable cardiac safety profile.

Pralsetinib.name: Pralsetinib
Pralsetinib.description: Pralsetinib is a selective RET tyrosine kinase inhibitor used to treat RET-fusion positive metastatic non-small cell lung cancer. It offers greater specificity for RET receptors compared to earlier multikinase inhibitors, reducing toxicity. Pralsetinib was granted accelerated FDA approval in 2020 and is marketed as GAVRETO™. Its key effect is targeted inhibition of RET-driven cancer cell growth.
Pralsetinib.mechanism_of_action: Pralsetinib is a selective inhibitor of the RET receptor tyrosine kinase, which is abnormally activated in certain cancers due to gene fusions. By binding to RET, pralsetinib blocks its kinase activity, preventing downstream signaling that drives tumor growth and proliferation. It demonstrates high selectivity for RET over most other kinases but can also inhibit several other kinases at clinical doses. The main therapeutic effect is attributed to RET inhibition, leading to anti-tumor activity.
Pralsetinib.pharmacodynamics: Pralsetinib specifically inhibits the RET tyrosine kinase, targeting various oncogenic RET fusions and mutations. Its high selectivity for RET results in effective anti-tumor activity and a better safety profile than older multi-kinase inhibitors. Clinically, pralsetinib can still cause adverse effects such as hypertension, hemorrhage, impaired wound healing, hepatotoxicity, lung disease, and embryo-fetal toxicity. Careful monitoring is recommended during treatment.

Vandetanib.name: Vandetanib
Vandetanib.description: Vandetanib is an oral, once-daily kinase inhibitor that targets tumor angiogenesis and cell proliferation. It is primarily indicated for adults with nonresectable, locally advanced, or metastatic medullary thyroid cancer. The drug works by inhibiting pathways involved in tumor growth and spread. Vandetanib may have potential use in other tumor types as well.
Vandetanib.mechanism_of_action: ZD-6474 inhibits the tyrosine kinases VEGFR, EGFR, and RET. By blocking VEGFR and EGFR, it disrupts key signaling pathways involved in cancer cell growth and angiogenesis. Inhibition of RET is particularly relevant for certain thyroid cancers. This multi-targeted approach underlies its use in cancers such as NSCLC and medullary thyroid cancer.
